Real-Time Retail Feedback Intelligence

Final Capstone Submission | ChicStyle GenAI-Powered Review Intelligence

Executive Summary

This project develops a real-time retail feedback intelligence system that transforms unstructured customer reviews into structured, actionable insights. By automatically classifying feedback, summarizing key issues, identifying sentiment and urgency, and drafting personalized responses, the solution helps customer support, merchandising, and operations teams respond faster during high-volume review periods.

The analysis shows that structured reasoning improves output quality and consistency. The strongest proposed production design combines retrieval-based context with structured reasoning, enabling the system to use similar historical reviews and approved responses as guidance while still producing review-specific outputs.

  • Convert large volumes of review text into standardized business signals.
  • Identify product, fit, quality, price, delivery, and other customer issues faster.
  • Support consistent customer communication through structured personalized messages.
  • Prepare the system for future retrieval-augmented deployment using historical response data.

Problem & Solution Summary

ChicStyle receives large volumes of customer reviews, especially during promotional and seasonal sales. Manual review monitoring is slow, inconsistent, and difficult to scale. Traditional keyword-based methods also miss nuance such as mixed sentiment, subtle fit issues, and context-specific quality concerns.

The proposed solution uses a GenAI-based review intelligence pipeline to classify each review, summarize the main customer experience, determine sentiment and urgency, and generate a concise response message. This creates a structured feedback layer that can support dashboards, escalation workflows, and customer service operations.

Model Comparison & Insights

When no reference is provided, evaluation compares predictions against internally derived expectations based on the evaluation framework. This makes reference-free evaluation useful for pairwise comparison between versions of the same technique. For absolute benchmarking across techniques, a labeled ground truth dataset is more appropriate, though maintaining such datasets at large scale may require historical response data.

Across prompting approaches, more structured techniques produce more reliable and consistent outputs. The overall performance ranking observed from the analysis is:

RAG + Structured Reasoning > Structured Reasoning > Few-Shot > Zero-Shot

Structured reasoning improves consistency by breaking the task into stages: identifying the main issue, selecting the correct category, determining sentiment, summarizing the review, and generating a response. Retrieval further improves the proposed production design by grounding outputs in similar historical examples or approved responses.

Final Solution Proposal

The final proposed solution is a structured GenAI review intelligence system with a future retrieval-enhanced layer. The current notebook demonstrates the core classification, summarization, sentiment, urgency, and response-generation workflow. RAG is included as a production enhancement proposal, not as a completed implementation inside the notebook.

  • Input layer: ingest customer reviews and product metadata such as rating, recommendation, division, department, and class.
  • Reasoning layer: apply structured prompting to identify the primary issue, sentiment, urgency, and customer-facing response.
  • Output layer: produce Category, Summary, Sentiment, Urgency, and PersonalizedMessage fields for downstream reporting and workflow use.
  • RAG enhancement: retrieve similar historical reviews and approved responses to improve consistency, reduce hallucination, and align responses with business policy.
  • Human oversight: review high-urgency, low-confidence, or customer-sensitive cases before customer-facing deployment.

Implementation Plan

Phase 1: Pilot Deployment

  • Deploy the structured prompting workflow on a representative review sample.
  • Validate outputs for category, sentiment, urgency, summary quality, and response usefulness.
  • Create dashboard-ready fields for business review.

Phase 2: Dashboard & Monitoring

  • Track review trends by category, sentiment, urgency, and product group.
  • Monitor repeated issues such as sizing, fabric quality, delivery delays, and price concerns.
  • Add quality checks for output consistency and escalation rules.

Phase 3: RAG Enhancement

  • Build a vector index of historical reviews, labeled outcomes, and approved response examples.
  • Retrieve top similar cases as reference guidance for structured generation.
  • Use retrieved context to improve consistency and brand-safe response quality.

Phase 4: Production Integration

  • Integrate the system with CRM, support, and analytics workflows.
  • Route high-urgency or sensitive reviews to human review.
  • Continuously improve the retrieval index and prompts based on feedback.

Estimated Benefits vs Costs

Estimated Benefits

  • 30–50% reduction in manual review triage effort by automating classification, summarization, and response drafting.
  • 2–3x faster identification of negative sentiment and urgent product issues during peak review periods.
  • More consistent response quality across customer support teams.
  • Earlier detection of recurring product issues that can inform merchandising and operations decisions.

Basis for Benefit Estimates

  • Assumes manual review triage typically requires several minutes per customer review.
  • Assumes high-volume retail review periods where automation can reduce repetitive reading and labeling work.
  • Assumes human review remains in place for high-risk, high-urgency, or customer-sensitive responses.
  • Future RAG and CRM integration may further improve consistency and response quality.

Development Costs

  • One-time pipeline development and testing: approximately 3,000–8,000 for a pilot implementation.
  • Dashboard and reporting setup: approximately 1,000–3,000 depending on integration depth.
  • RAG knowledge-base preparation and vector index setup: approximately 2,000–6,000 depending on data quality and volume.

Operational Costs

  • LLM API usage: approximately 200–1,000 per month depending on review volume, model choice, and prompt size.
  • Cloud storage and compute: approximately 100–300 per month for pilot to early-scale usage.
  • Ongoing monitoring and prompt maintenance: approximately 100–500 per month depending on governance requirements.

Estimated per-review processing cost is approximately 0.002–0.01 for lightweight classification and generation workflows, depending on prompt length, model selection, and whether retrieval context is included.

Risks & Mitigation

  • Risk: incorrect classification or summary. Mitigation: evaluation against labeled samples and human review for high-risk cases.
  • Risk: hallucinated or overconfident responses. Mitigation: retrieval grounding, approved response templates, and escalation rules.
  • Risk: inconsistent tone across responses. Mitigation: structured prompt rules and brand-approved message guidelines.
  • Risk: cost growth at scale. Mitigation: batching, model selection, prompt optimization, and selective use of RAG for cases that need grounding.

Conclusion

The project demonstrates that GenAI can convert unstructured retail reviews into useful business intelligence. A structured reasoning pipeline provides a strong foundation for classification, summarization, sentiment analysis, urgency detection, and response drafting. For production, adding RAG as a future enhancement will improve grounding, consistency, and traceability while enabling scalable customer feedback operations.

Real-Time Retail Feedback Intelligence

Business Context

ChicStyle — a growing fashion retail platform — experiences massive spikes in customer activity during festive seasons and holiday sales. As people buy clothing and accessories for celebrations, the volume of incoming reviews increases drastically. These reviews pour in every hour, ranging from positive praise to urgent complaints about fit, delivery delays, product defects, or sizing issues.During such high-pressure periods, even a slight delay in reading or responding to customer feedback can have serious consequences. If the retail team fails to take quick action, customers may feel ignored during an emotionally significant time (festive purchases), leading to frustration, spoiled shopping experiences, and ultimately reduced trust in the brand. This not only results in immediate business loss but also impacts repeat purchases and long-term loyalty. To avoid customer dissatisfaction and protect brand reputation during peak sales periods, ChicStyle needs a system that can process thousands of reviews instantly, accurately, and with business context.

Problem Statement

Retailers need a smart feedback analysis system that can automatically process large volumes of real-time customer reviews, accurately detect sentiment (positive, negative, neutral), identify which product or service the feedback is about, and highlight urgent or high-impact issues.Traditional NLP models often struggle with this because they depend on fixed rules and limited training data. They can’t easily understand complex or mixed feedback. For example, in the review “The fit is great but the color was not as per the product image,” older systems may give only one sentiment, either positive or negative, missing that the review talks positively about the fit but negatively about the color. Generative AI models are much better at this. They can understand the full meaning of a sentence, separate opinions about diﬀerent products, and catch mixed emotions. This helps retailers get more accurate insights and respond to customer problems faster.

Objective

Build a Generative AI feedback system that uses prompt engineering(Zero-Shot,Few-Shot,and Chain-of-Thought prompting)to:

  • Analyze and categorize sentiment in real time.
  • Detect which product or service each feedback refers to.
  • Summarize insights by product category and urgency level.
  • Automatically send short personalized messages to customers based on sentiment—thanking them for positive feedback, acknowledging neutral comments,and apologizing for negative ones while informing them that a team member will reach out soon.
  • Generate short, actionable reports for retail teams.This intelligent system will help retailers take quick action on customer issues,improve product quality faster,and enhance customer satisfaction—turning massive unstructured feedback into meaningful,real-time business intelligence.

Dataset Used for the Notebook

This analysis uses the "Women's E-Commerce Clothing Reviews" dataset. The key columns for the analysis are:

  • Clothing.ID: A unique ID for each piece of clothing.
  • Age: The age of the reviewer (Positive Integer).
  • Title: The title of the review (String).
  • Review.Text: The main body of the customer's review text (String).
  • Rating: The product score given by the customer, from 1 (Worst) to 5 (Best) (Positive Ordinal Integer).
  • Recommended.IND: A binary variable indicating if the customer recommends the product (1 for recommended, 0 for not recommended).
  • Positive.Feedback.Count: The number of other customers who found the review helpful(Positive Integer).
  • Division.Name: The high-level division of the product (Categorical).
  • Department.Name: The specific department of the product (Categorical).
  • Class.Name: The clothing item's class name (Categorical)

Data Cleaning and Preprocessing

Think about it: The Review Text column is the most critical feature for our Generative AI model. What should be done with rows where this text is missing?

Two options:

  • Ignore reviews missing text if only small percentage without text. If a customer does not leave a review, it is likely the customer does not expect a post-sale response. Will follow this option in the milestone submission. In the final submission, plan to add specific instruction to treat no-comment reviews.
  • Impute a default value, i.e. "No comment" as input to LLM. This can be done without modifying dataframe itself. Rather can enforce the rule in LLM processing logic.

Observation:

  • There are 845 records without review, less than 5% of the total records.
  • For all no-review records, Positive Feedback Count is zero. This is expected as empty review does not help any other customer.
  • Out of 845 records without review, only 1 record has title.
  • Out of 845 records without review, 71 records do not recommend the purchased items, 0.3%(71/23486) of the total records. Rest of the no-review records all recommended the purchased item. Hence we recommend to drop the no-review record as most of them, >=91.5% (845-71)/845, do recommend the purchased items. Also given that it is difficult to follow up on no-review feedbacks, it is recommended to exclude no-review record in the scope of this project. In production scenario, can add strict and specific processing rules in the prompt for such reviews. Related example rules will be added in final submission.

Exploratory Data Analysis

We will answer the following questions with EDA

  1. What is the summary statistics of the numerical data? What can you infer about the distribution of Age, Rating, and Positive Feedback Count?

  2. How many unique values are there in the categorical columns like Division Name, Department Name, and Class Name?

  3. What is the overall distribution of product Rating? Is the dataset skewed towards positive or negative reviews?

  4. Which Department Name receives the highest average rating, and which receives the lowest? What might this indicate?

  5. What are the most common words found in highly-rated reviews (4-5 stars) versus poorly-rated reviews (1-2 stars)? What initial hypotheses can you form about the key drivers of customer satisfaction and dissatisfaction?

In [13]:
def histogram_boxplot_ax(feature, ax_box, ax_hist, bins="auto"):
    """ Boxplot and histogram combined
    feature: 1-d feature array
    figsize: size of fig (default (15, 10))
    bins: number of bins (default "auto")
    """
    # Creating the subplots
    # Boxplot will be created and the mean value of the column will be indicated using some symbol
    sns.boxplot(x=feature, ax=ax_box, showmeans=True, color='red')
    sns.histplot(x=feature, kde=False, ax=ax_hist, bins=bins)

    # Add mean and median to the histogram
    ax_hist.axvline(np.mean(feature), color='g', linestyle='--')
    ax_hist.axvline(np.median(feature), color='black', linestyle='-')


def plot_tiled_combo(df, columns, nrows, ncols, bins="auto"):
    from matplotlib.gridspec import GridSpec, GridSpecFromSubplotSpec

    fig = plt.figure(figsize=(3*ncols, 2*nrows))
    outer = GridSpec(nrows, ncols, figure=fig)

    for idx, col in enumerate(columns):
        r = idx // ncols
        c = idx % ncols

        # Create inner grid (box + hist) inside each tile
        inner = GridSpecFromSubplotSpec(
            2, 1,
            subplot_spec=outer[r, c],
            height_ratios=[1, 3]
        )

        ax_box = fig.add_subplot(inner[0])
        ax_hist = fig.add_subplot(inner[1], sharex=ax_box)
        ax_hist.set_title('')
        histogram_boxplot_ax(df[col], ax_box, ax_hist, bins)
        ax_box.set_title(col)
        ax_box.set_xlabel('')

    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()

Observation

  • Age has a rather symmetrical distribution, a wide range of values from 18-99 and slightly skewed to the right.
  • Median value of Rating is 5. In fact >=75% of the rating values are 5. The distribution is concentrated in value 5, but skewed to the left, low ratings.
  • Median of PositiveFeedbackCount is 1, with max of 122, heavily skewed to the right.

Observation

How many unique values are there in the categorical columns like Division Name, Department Name, and Class Name?

  • 3 unique division names: 'Initmates', 'General', 'General Petite'
  • 6 unique department names: 'Intimate', 'Dresses', 'Bottoms', 'Tops', 'Jackets', 'Trend'
  • 20 unique classe names: 'Intimates', 'Dresses', 'Pants', 'Blouses', 'Knits', 'Outerwear', 'Lounge', 'Sweaters', 'Skirts', 'Fine gauge', 'Sleep', 'Jackets', 'Swim', 'Trend', 'Jeans', 'Legwear', 'Shorts', 'Layering', 'Casual bottoms', 'Chemises'
In [17]:
display(df_has_review.groupby('Rating').size())
# rating By Division, Department, ClassName
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(12, 3))
# ----------- 1. Department -----------
sns.violinplot(x="Department", y="Rating",data=df_has_review, ax=axes[0])
dept_order = ['Intimate', 'Dresses', 'Bottoms', 'Tops', 'Jackets', 'Trend']
grouped_dept = df_has_review.groupby('Department')['Rating']
for i, dept in enumerate(dept_order):
    if dept in grouped_dept.mean():
        axes[0].scatter(i, grouped_dept.mean()[dept], marker='D')
axes[0].set_title("Rating by Department")
axes[0].tick_params(axis='x', rotation=45, labelsize=7)

# ----------- 2. Division -----------
sns.violinplot(x="Division", y="Rating",data=df_has_review, ax=axes[1])
grouped_div = df_has_review.groupby('Division')['Rating']
div_order = df_has_review['Division'].unique()
for i, div in enumerate(div_order):
    if div in grouped_div.mean():
        axes[1].scatter(i, grouped_div.mean()[div], marker='D')
axes[1].set_title("Rating by Division")
axes[1].tick_params(axis='x', rotation=45, labelsize=7)

# ----------- 3. ClassName -----------
sns.violinplot(x="ClassName", y="Rating",data=df_has_review, ax=axes[2])
# Optional: limit to top N classes for readability
top_classes = df_has_review['ClassName'].value_counts().head(12).index
df_top_class = df_has_review[df_has_review['ClassName'].isin(top_classes)]
grouped_class = df_top_class.groupby('ClassName')['Rating']
for i, cls in enumerate(top_classes):
    if cls in grouped_class.mean():
        axes[2].scatter(i, grouped_class.mean()[cls], marker='D')
axes[2].set_title("Rating by ClassName (Top 12)")
axes[2].tick_params(axis='x', rotation=45, labelsize=7)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
0
Rating
1 821
2 1549
3 2823
4 4908
5 12540

Observation

Which Department Name receives the highest average rating, and which receives the lowest? What might this indicate?

  • Intimate, Bottoms, Jackets tend to receive high average rating whereas Dresses, Tops and Trend tend to receive low average rating.
  • The above observation is reasonable since buyers of Intimate, Bottoms, Jackets pay more attention to functionalities than to appearance while people focus more on appearance for Dresses, Tops and Trend, with Trend being the most appearance-focused department. This observation is also confirmed by mean/median comparison across class names.
  • There is no significant difference in rating across three divisions.
In [18]:
# Convert Recommended to integer column Recommendation
df_has_review['Recommendation'] = df_has_review.Recommended.astype(int)
num_var = ['Age', 'Rating', 'PositiveFeedbackCount', 'Recommendation']
corr = df_has_review[num_var].corr()
plt.figure(figsize=(4, 3))
ax = sns.heatmap(corr, annot = True, cmap = 'Spectral',fmt = ".1f", xticklabels = corr.columns, yticklabels = corr.columns)
ax.set_title('Correlation HeatMap')
ax.tick_params(axis='x', labelsize=8, rotation=15)
ax.tick_params(axis='y', labelsize=8)

Observation:

  • There is strong correlation between Rating and Recommendation, a 0.8 positive correlation.
  • There is weak negative correlation of -0.1 for the pair of Recommendation & PositiveFeedbackCount and the pair of Rating and PositiveFeedbackCount. This can be interpreted as customers tend to pay more attention to low-rating and no-recommendation reviews and consider them to be more helpful, a suggestion for LLM model to prioritize low-rating reviews without recommendation.
In [19]:
def plot_wordcloud(ax, text_list, title, stopwords, background_color="white"):
    """
    Generate and plot a word cloud from a list of text strings.
    Parameters:
    text_list (list of str): input text data
    figsize (tuple): figure size
    background_color (str): background color of word cloud
    """
    # 1. Combine all text
    text = " ".join(str(t) for t in text_list)
    # 2. Generate word cloud
    wc = WordCloud(width=300,height=200,background_color=background_color,stopwords=stopwords).generate(text)
    # 3. Plot on axis
    ax.imshow(wc, interpolation="bilinear")
    ax.axis("off")
    ax.set_title(title)
In [20]:
# using WordCloud to find top words used in both high-rating and low-rating reviews.
fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(8, 6))
# Start with default stopwords
stopwords = set(STOPWORDS)
plot_wordcloud(axes[0,0], np.array(df_has_review[df_has_review.Rating>=4].Review), "High Rating", stopwords)
plot_wordcloud(axes[0,1], np.array(df_has_review[df_has_review.Rating<=2].Review), "Low Rating", stopwords)

# add custom stopwords to exclude commonly used netural words in review.
# the motivation is to make sentiment related words more prominent.
extra_stopwords = ['dress','color','fabric','shirt','top', 'size', 'ordered','bought', 'wear', 'look', 'fit', 'looks', 'looked',
                   'much','will', 'material', 'one']
stopwords.update(extra_stopwords)
plot_wordcloud(axes[1,0], np.array(df_has_review[df_has_review.Rating>=4].Review), "High Rating - custom stopwords", stopwords)
plot_wordcloud(axes[1,1], np.array(df_has_review[df_has_review.Rating<=2].Review), "Low Rating - custom stopwords", stopwords)

Observation

What are the most common words found in highly-rated reviews (4-5 stars) versus poorly-rated reviews (1-2 stars)? (Hint: Use Word Clouds). What initial hypotheses can you form about the key drivers of customer satisfaction and dissatisfaction?

  • "love", "great", "perfect", "comfortable", "flattering", "beautiful" are among the top words for highly-reated reviews. "love", "small", "large", "material", "unfortunately", "returned", "disappointed" are among the top words for low-rating reviews although some top words for highly-rating reviews also appear in these low-rated reviews as top words, suggesting that there is often mixed sentiment in low-rating reviews and words of negative sentiment count more than positive ones.
  • From the Word Cloud using default stopword set, size/fit and look/color for tops and dresses are key drivers for both customer satisfaction and dissatisfaction.

Building the Generative AI Pipeline

We will now build a system to analyze the reviews. This involves setting up the AI client, designing prompts, generating structured data, and evaluating the results.

For this project, we will analyze and categorize a sample of 50-100 customer reviews. This number is chosen intentionally. Since the API has a budget limit of $20, running prompts on very large datasets can quickly exhaust your quota—especially because this exercise may involve multiple iterations, prompt refinements, and repeated evaluations.

To avoid unnecessary cost and ensure efficient experimentation, we recommend the following approach:

  • Use very small samples (5–10 reviews) during the initial testing phase to validate your prompt structure and logic.

  • Scale up to 100 reviews for the final evaluation phase, ensuring enough data to compare prompting techniques without draining your budget.

  • This strategy helps maintain cost control while still providing meaningful insights across Zero-Shot, Few-Shot, and Chain-of-Thought techniques.

Setup AI Client

In [21]:
import os
from google.colab import userdata   # Colab helper to read secrets
from openai import OpenAI            # OpenAI-compatible client for OpenAI
# Setting up the OPENAI Client
# Read the OpenAI API key from Colab Secrets (secret name must match exactly)
mygreatlearnin_endpoint = "https://aibe.mygreatlearning.com/openai/v1"
openai_api_key = userdata.get("OPEN_AI_KEY")   # retrieve this secret in Colab as described above
personal_openai_api_key = userdata.get("OPEN_AI_KEY_PERSONAL")
openai_endpoint = "https://api.openai.com/v1"

# Instantiate the OpenAI-compatible client pointing to mygreatlearning endpoint
# This is only for connection and quota check. We will use langchain ChatOpenAI class and syntax for actual processing
api_key = openai_api_key
base_url = mygreatlearnin_endpoint
api_key = personal_openai_api_key
base_url = openai_endpoint
client = OpenAI(
    api_key=api_key,
    base_url=base_url
)

print("OpenAI client created. You can now use `client` to call embeddings and chat endpoints.")
# List available models
print("Available Models (sample):")
models = client.models.list()
model_ids = list(filter(lambda x: x.startswith("gpt-4"), [m.id for m in models.data]))
print(model_ids, "\n")

['gpt-4-613', 'gpt-4', 'gpt-4-turbo', 'gpt-4-turbo-2024-04-09', 'gpt-4o', 'gpt-4o-2024-05-13', 'gpt-4o-mini-2024-07-18', 'gpt-4o-mini', 'gpt-4o-2024-08-06', 'gpt-4o-audio-preview', 'gpt-4o-realtime-preview', 'gpt-4o-realtime-preview-2024-12-17', 'gpt-4o-audio-preview-2024-12-17', 'gpt-4o-mini-realtime-preview-2024-12-17', 'gpt-4o-mini-audio-preview-2024-12-17', 'gpt-4o-mini-realtime-preview', 'gpt-4o-mini-audio-preview', 'gpt-4o-2024-11-20', 'gpt-4o-mini-search-preview-2025-03-11', 'gpt-4o-mini-search-preview', 'gpt-4o-transcribe', 'gpt-4o-mini-transcribe', 'gpt-4o-mini-tts', 'gpt-4.1-2025-04-14', 'gpt-4.1', 'gpt-4.1-mini-2025-04-14', 'gpt-4.1-mini', 'gpt-4.1-nano-2025-04-14', 'gpt-4.1-nano', 'gpt-4o-realtime-preview-2025-06-03', 'gpt-4o-audio-preview-2025-06-03', 'gpt-4o-transcribe-diarize', 'gpt-4o-mini-transcribe-2025-12-15', 'gpt-4o-mini-transcribe-2025-03-20', 'gpt-4o-mini-tts-2025-03-20', 'gpt-4o-mini-tts-2025-12-15', 'gpt-4o-search-preview', 'gpt-4o-search-preview-2025-03-11']

LangChain tool setup

We will use langchain OpenAI compatible interfaces for calling LLM service.

In [22]:
# to use LangChain LCEL syntax, instantiate llm instance here
from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI
from langchain_core.prompts import ChatPromptTemplate
from langchain_core.output_parsers import JsonOutputParser
# gpt-4.1 → part of the newer “4.x” iteration (more recent update in the GPT-4 line)
# gpt-4o → “o” stands for omni (multimodal focus: text, image, audio), not a version number
#model_name = 'gpt-4o'
model_name = 'gpt-4.1' # we choose this model as it better handles LLM judge prompt with longer context
temperature = 0
llm = ChatOpenAI(model=model_name,temperature=temperature,api_key=api_key,base_url=base_url)

Prompt Engineering and Evaluation

Based on the previous milestone submission and findings, we apply both CoT to LLM judge and review prompt with additional updates to improve both prediction and evaluation performance.

Create a LLM-as-a-judge class LlmJudge to evaluate the quality of model outputs

In [23]:
from contextlib import contextmanager
import time

@contextmanager
def ProcessTimer(name="Block"):
    start = time.perf_counter()
    yield
    end = time.perf_counter()
    print(f"{name} took {end - start:.6f} seconds")

class Utils:
    """
    Utility/helper functions for LLM-based processing classes.

    Responsible for:
    - Converting pandas DataFrame rows into LangChain input format
    - Batch conversion of DataFrames to input dictionaries
    - Converting LLM results into structured pandas DataFrames

    This separation keeps LLM-based processing classes focused on LLM execution logic.
    """

    @staticmethod
    def df_row_to_langchain_input(df, idx, column_map=None, default_map=None, global_default="unknown"):
        """
        Convert a DataFrame row into a LangChain-compatible input dictionary
        using flexible column mapping and per-key default values.

        Parameters:
        ----------
        df : pd.DataFrame
        idx : int
            Row index

        column_map : dict, optional
            Mapping of output keys → DataFrame column names

        default_map : dict, optional
            Mapping of output keys → default values

            Example:
            {
                "title": "",
                "review": "No comment",
                "rating": 0
            }

        global_default : Any
            Fallback default if key not present in default_map (default: "unknown")

        Returns:
        -------
        dict
            LangChain input dictionary
        """

        row = df.iloc[idx]

        # ---- Default map ----
        if default_map is None:
            default_map = {}

        def safe_get(out_key, col_name):
            default_val = default_map.get(out_key, global_default)
            if col_name not in row:
                return default_val
            val = row.get(col_name, default_val)
            if pd.isna(val):
                return default_val
            return val

        # ---- Build output ----
        return {out_key: safe_get(out_key, col_name) for col_name, out_key in column_map.items()}

    @staticmethod
    def df_to_langchain_inputs(df, column_map, default_map=None):
        return [
            Utils.df_row_to_langchain_input(df, i, column_map=column_map, default_map=default_map)
            for i in range(len(df))
        ]

    @staticmethod
    def results_to_dataframe(results, index):
        """
        Convert processing results into a normalized pandas DataFrame.

        Parameters:
        ----------
        results : list
        index : pandas Index

        Returns:
        -------
        pd.DataFrame
        """
        rows = []

        for res in results:
            if res["success"]:
                row = res["result"]
                row["success"] = True
                row["error"] = None
            else:
                row = {
                    "success": False,
                    "error": res["error"]
                }
            rows.append(row)

        df_out = pd.json_normalize(rows)
        df_out.index = index

        return df_out

class LlmJudge:
    """
    LLM-as-a-Judge evaluator with rate limiting and Jupyter progress support.
    If reference values exist for reference columns in a dataframe, it uses
    reference values as ground truth for evaluation. Otherwise, internally
    inferred values are used.
    """

    WEIGHTS = {
        "Category": 0.2,
        "Sentiment": 0.2,
        "Summary": 0.2,
        "PersonalizedMessage": 0.2,
        "RetailInsight": 0.2
    }

    def __init__(self, llm_judge_prompt, input_cols, prediction_cols,
                 input_key_map=None, prediction_key_map=None,
                 reference_cols=None, reference_key_map=None,
                 model_name="gpt-4o",
                 temperature=0, api_key=None, base_url=None,
                 calls=10, period=60):

        self.llm = ChatOpenAI(
            model=model_name,
            temperature=temperature,
            api_key=api_key,
            base_url=base_url
        )
        self.input_cols = input_cols
        self.prediction_cols = prediction_cols
        self.input_key_map = input_key_map
        self.prediction_key_map = prediction_key_map
        self.reference_cols = reference_cols
        self.reference_key_map = reference_key_map

        self.parser = JsonOutputParser()
        self.chain = llm_judge_prompt | self.llm | self.parser

        # ---- rate limit config ----
        self.calls = calls
        self.period = period

    # --------------------------------------------------
    # RATE LIMITED WRAPPER
    # --------------------------------------------------
    def _invoke_chain(self, payload):

        @sleep_and_retry
        @limits(calls=self.calls, period=self.period)
        def call():
            return self.chain.invoke(payload)

        return call()

    def evaluate_one(self, row_input: dict, prediction: dict, reference: dict):
        """
        Single LLM evaluation call (rate-limited).
        """

        result = self._invoke_chain({**row_input,**prediction, **reference})

        scores = result.get("scores", {})

        inferred_values = result.get("inferred", {})

        score_reasons = result.get("score_reasons", {})

        final_score = 0
        for k, w in self.WEIGHTS.items():
            s = float(scores.get(k, 0))
            s = min(max(s, 0), 1)
            final_score += w * s

        return {
            "scores": scores,
            "final_score": final_score,
            "inferred": inferred_values,
            "score_reasons": score_reasons,
            "record_id": row_input.get("record_id", None)
        }

    # --------------------------------------------------
    # BATCH EVALUATION WITH PROGRESS BAR
    # --------------------------------------------------
    def evaluate_batch(self, df, show_progress=True):

        results = []

        if self.input_key_map is None:
            self.input_key_map = {c: c for c in self.input_cols}

        if self.prediction_key_map is None:
            self.prediction_key_map = {c: c for c in self.prediction_cols}

        if self.reference_key_map is None:
            self.reference_key_map = {c: c for c in self.reference_cols}

        if show_progress:
            try:
                from tqdm.notebook import tqdm
            except ImportError:
                from tqdm import tqdm
            iterator = tqdm(df.iterrows(), total=len(df), desc="Evaluating with LLM Judge")
        else:
            iterator = df.iterrows()

        running_score = 0

        for i, (idx, row) in enumerate(iterator):

            row_input = {
                self.input_key_map[col]: row[col]
                for col in self.input_cols
            }

            pred = {
                self.prediction_key_map[col]: row[col]
                for col in self.prediction_cols
            }

            reference = {}
            if self.reference_cols and self.reference_key_map:
                for col in self.reference_cols:
                    key = self.reference_key_map.get(col, col)
                    val = row.get(col, "")
                    # Handle NaN / None
                    if pd.isna(val):
                        val = ""
                    reference[key] = val

            res = self.evaluate_one(row_input, pred, reference)
            res["index"] = idx

            results.append(res)
            running_score += res["final_score"]

            if show_progress:
                iterator.set_postfix(avg_score=running_score / (i + 1))

        avg_score = running_score / len(results) if results else 0

        return {
            "average_score": avg_score,
            "details": results
        }
In [24]:
LLM_JUDGE_PROMPT = ChatPromptTemplate.from_messages([
("system",
"""
You are an expert evaluator for a customer review analysis system for
ChicStyle, a fashion retail platform.
Your task is to evaluate the quality of predicted structured outputs.
====================================================
CORE EVALUATION PRINCIPLES
====================================================
1. First infer the best correct value for each field using ONLY the
INPUT.
2. Then compare each predicted field against its inferred field.
3. Evaluate each predicted field independently.
4. Do NOT reuse content from one predicted field to score another
predicted field.
5. All scores MUST be clamped to the range [0.0, 1.0].
====================================================
REFERENCE-BASED SCORING (OVERRIDE LOGIC)
====================================================
Optional reference values may be provided for:
- Category
- Sentiment
- Summary
- PersonalizedMessage
- Urgency

A reference value is considered EMPTY if it is blank, whitespace only, null, None, NaN, N/A, or "".

SCORING RULE (MANDATORY):
For EACH field independently:

IF reference value is NOT EMPTY:
- Use the reference value as the scoring target for that field.
- The "inferred" output for that field MUST reflect the reference value.
- DO NOT replace a non-empty reference with your own inferred value.

IF reference value IS EMPTY:
- Use the standard inference process.
- Infer the correct value from INPUT.
- Compare predicted value against the inferred value.

FIELD-SPECIFIC REFERENCE SCORING:
1. Category, Sentiment, Urgency
 - Score by exact normalized label match against the reference.
 - If the reference label is invalid, fall back to standard inference for that field.

2. Summary
 - Score by semantic equivalence to ref_summary, not exact wording.
 - Award full credit when the predicted summary preserves the same product, main issue/praise, context, and objective tone.
 - Do not penalize harmless paraphrasing.
 - Penalize only when the prediction changes, omits, exaggerates, or contradicts the reference's main meaning.

3. PersonalizedMessage
 - Score by semantic equivalence to ref_personalized_message, not exact wording.
 - Award full credit when the predicted message preserves brand mention, issue/praise relevance, tone, and follow-up/actionability when applicable.
 - Do not penalize harmless paraphrasing.
 - Penalize only when the prediction omits ChicStyle, loses the main issue/praise, changes the tone, or drops the needed action.

CRITICAL RULES:
1. Apply this logic independently per field.
2. Some fields may use reference while others use inference.
3. NEVER mix reference and inferred values for the same field.
4. Reference values are authoritative for scoring when non-empty and valid.
5. For Summary and PersonalizedMessage, semantic equivalence is enough; exact wording is NOT required.

FINAL PRIORITY:
valid non-empty reference > inferred value
====================================================
CRITICAL FIELD ISOLATION RULE
====================================================
When scoring a field, use ONLY:
- the INPUT
- the scoring target for that same field (reference if non-empty and valid; otherwise inferred value)
- the predicted value for that same field
For PersonalizedMessage:
- You MUST evaluate ONLY the literal predicted message:
Predicted PersonalizedMessage = {predicted_personalized_message}
STRICT RULES:
- Do NOT use system prompt patterns as evidence
- Do NOT infer that a missing phrase is "implicitly present"
- If a value is not explicitly present in the predicted field, treat it
as ABSENT
- Do NOT copy evidence from Category, Summary, or other predicted fields
into PersonalizedMessage scoring
This is a CLOSED-BOOK evaluation per field.
====================================================
CRITICAL SCORING SAFETY RULE
====================================================
FINAL SCORE CONSTRAINT (MANDATORY):
- All scores MUST be between 0.0 and 1.0
- If computed score < 0.0 -> set to 0.0
- If computed score > 1.0 -> set to 1.0
- Negative numbers are NOT allowed
====================================================
INFERENCE PROTOCOL
====================================================
Infer the correct field values using ONLY:
- Review Title
- Review Text
- Rating
- Recommended

HOWEVER:
- If a reference value exists for a field, inference is OPTIONAL for that field
- Inference is REQUIRED ONLY when reference is empty
====================================================
CATEGORY INFERENCE RULES
====================================================
Allowed labels:
["Delivery", "Fit", "Quality", "Price", "Other"]
Infer Category using these STRICT rules:
1. Delivery
- Use if the review complains about shipping delay, late arrival,
logistics, packaging, returns, or customer service
- Delivery overrides ALL other categories IF logistics/shipping is
mentioned negatively
2. Fit
- Use if the main issue is sizing, tightness, looseness, comfort, runs
small/large
- Fit overrides Quality if both fit and quality are mentioned
3. Quality
- Use if the main issue/praise is fabric, material, stitching,
durability, craftsmanship, or defects
- Only use Quality when Fit is not the dominant issue
4. Price
- Use if the main issue/praise is price, affordability, value,
overpriced, or worth it
5. Other
- Use only if none of the above clearly applies
If multiple issues are present:
- choose the PRIMARY cause of dissatisfaction or praise
- secondary comments do not control the label


====================================================
CATEGORY DOMINANCE CLARIFICATION (v6)
====================================================
The Category rules above should be interpreted using PRIMARY DRIVER, not keyword presence alone.

Fit vs Quality:
- Fit does NOT automatically override Quality merely because a fit-related word appears.
- Fit overrides Quality ONLY when the fit, sizing, silhouette, comfort, or body-wearability issue is the dominant cause of satisfaction, dissatisfaction, return, or recommendation.
- Quality should remain the inferred category when material, construction, durability, appearance, design execution, color, sheerness, wash outcome, or craftsmanship is the dominant cause.
- When both Fit and Quality are explicitly present and both are plausible, infer the preferred category from the customerâ€™s final outcome:
  -> return/dissatisfaction caused by body wearability = Fit
  -> return/dissatisfaction caused by material/appearance/product execution = Quality
  -> praise caused by fit/flattering/wearability = Fit
  -> praise caused by material/design/color/style/product attributes = Quality

Other:
- Other remains a last-resort category.
- Use Other only when Delivery, Fit, Quality, and Price do not clearly apply.
- Do not use Other when the review contains a defensible Fit, Quality, Delivery, or Price driver.

Ambiguity:
- Fashion reviews can contain subjective overlap. When two adjacent labels are both defensible from explicit review evidence, this should be treated as an ambiguity case for scoring rather than a completely unrelated error.

====================================================
CATEGORY SCORING
====================================================
REFERENCE OVERRIDE:
- If ref_category is NOT EMPTY: -> treat ref_category as inferred category
STEP 1 - VALIDITY
Predicted Category is valid if and only if it EXACTLY matches one of:
Fit, Quality, Delivery, Price, Other
STEP 2 - NORMALIZATION
Use trimmed versions of predicted and inferred category for exact
comparison.
STEP 3 - SCORING
- If predicted category is invalid -> score 0.0
- If valid and exact match to inferred -> score 1.0
- If valid but not equal to inferred -> score 0.0
CATEGORY REASON RULES
- If score = 1.0 -> reason MUST indicate correct category match
- If invalid -> reason MUST indicate invalid label and allowed set
- If valid but incorrect -> reason MUST indicate expected inferred
category
- Do NOT contradict score and reason

====================================================
CATEGORY PARTIAL-CREDIT SCORING OVERRIDE (v6)
====================================================
This section refines CATEGORY SCORING while preserving exact-match scoring.

After STEP 3, apply this additional rule for valid predicted Category labels:

- Exact match to inferred/reference category -> score 1.0
- Valid but not exact, AND the predicted category is supported by explicit review evidence in an ambiguous fashion-review case -> score 0.5
- Valid but not exact, AND the predicted category is not supported by explicit review evidence or is unrelated to the primary driver -> score 0.0

Ambiguous category pairs eligible for partial credit:
- Fit vs Quality, when the review explicitly discusses both body wearability and material/product appearance
- Quality vs Other, when the review discusses product attributes and general usability/occasion suitability
- Fit vs Other, when the review discusses body wearability and general usability/coverage
- Price vs Quality, when value/cost is tied directly to product material/design quality
- Delivery vs Other, when customer-service/return-process wording is ambiguous but service context is explicit

Partial credit should NOT be awarded when:
- a valid non-empty reference category is provided and the predicted label does not match it
- the predicted category ignores the only explicit issue or praise
- the predicted category is based on a secondary issue while the primary driver is clear
- the predicted category contradicts the review text

CATEGORY REASON RULES FOR PARTIAL CREDIT:
- If score = 0.5, reason MUST state:
  1. the preferred inferred/reference category
  2. why the predicted category is still partially supported by explicit evidence
- If the reason says the predicted category matches the inferred/reference category, the score MUST be 1.0.
- If the score is 0.0, the reason MUST clearly identify the different expected category and why the predicted category is not sufficiently supported.

====================================================
SENTIMENT INFERENCE RULES
====================================================
Allowed labels:
["Positive", "Negative", "Neutral"]
Infer Sentiment using these STRICT rules:
Primary rule:
- Text sentiment is the strongest signal
Definitions:
- Positive -> clear satisfaction, praise, strong approval, recommendation
- Negative -> complaint, dissatisfaction, frustration, strong disapproval
- Neutral -> mixed feedback OR factual feedback without strong emotional
dominance
Decision rules:
1. Strong negative language -> Negative regardless of rating
2. Strong positive language -> Positive
3. Mixed sentiment:
- complaint dominates -> Negative
- praise dominates -> Positive
- otherwise -> Neutral
Rating-based support:
- Rating <= 2 -> default toward Negative unless clearly contradicted by
text
- Rating = 3 -> likely Neutral unless strong text sentiment is present
- Rating >= 4 -> default toward Positive if language supports it
Recommended flag support:
- Recommended = 0 -> supports Negative
- Recommended = 1 -> supports Positive
Final rule:
- Clear text sentiment overrides rating and recommendation
====================================================
SENTIMENT SCORING
====================================================
REFERENCE OVERRIDE:
- If ref_sentiment is NOT EMPTY:
 -> treat ref_sentiment as inferred sentiment
Validity:
Predicted Sentiment is valid if and only if it EXACTLY matches one of:
Positive, Negative, Neutral
Scoring:
- If invalid -> 0.0
- If exact match to inferred -> 1.0
- Otherwise -> 0.0
REASON RULES
- If score = 1.0 -> reason MUST indicate correct sentiment match
- If invalid -> reason MUST indicate invalid label and allowed set
- If incorrect -> reason MUST indicate expected inferred sentiment
====================================================
SUMMARY INFERENCE RULES
====================================================
Infer a reference-quality summary using the generation prompt rules:
The inferred summary should:
- Be no more than 2 sentences
- Mention the product type when reasonably available from the input
- Include the key issue or praise
- Include context such as fit, quality, delivery, or price when relevant
- Be concise, factual, and non-repetitive
- Avoid speculation, exaggeration, or unsupported claims
Preferred structure:
- Sentence 1: product + key experience
- Sentence 2 (optional): specific issue or additional context
====================================================
SUMMARY SCORING
====================================================
REFERENCE OVERRIDE:
- If ref_summary is NOT EMPTY:
 -> treat ref_summary as the scoring target and inferred Summary
 -> evaluate predicted Summary against ref_summary by semantic equivalence
 -> do NOT require exact wording

Evaluate the predicted Summary on four dimensions:
1. Product Reference
- 1 if the product is clearly referenced or matches the reference's product meaning
- 0 if missing or too vague
2. Key Issue/Praise
- 1 if the main praise or complaint in the scoring target is captured
- 0 if missing, contradicted, or materially changed
3. Context Accuracy
- 1 if the relevant context/category in the scoring target is captured correctly
- 0 if missing or materially misleading
4. Tone/Objectivity
- 1 if concise, factual, non-speculative, and not emotionally exaggerated
- 0 if subjective, speculative, or noticeably inflated
Scoring formula:
Summary score =
0.25 * Product Reference +
0.35 * Key Issue/Praise +
0.25 * Context Accuracy +
0.15 * Tone/Objectivity
Exact-match / semantic-equivalence shortcut:
- If predicted summary is materially equivalent to the scoring target and satisfies all rules -> score 1.0
Important:
- Do NOT require verbatim wording
- Evaluate semantic correctness, not exact phrasing
- A summary may earn partial credit even if phrased differently from the scoring target
- When ref_summary is provided, do not penalize the prediction for not matching your own inferred wording
SUMMARY REASON RULES
- Explain which components are present or missing relative to the scoring target
- If score = 1.0, reason should indicate complete and accurate summary coverage

====================================================
SUMMARY SEMANTIC SCORING CLARIFICATION (v6)
====================================================
The semantic-equivalence principle applies both with and without reference summaries.

When ref_summary is EMPTY:
- Infer the scoring target from the INPUT.
- Do NOT require the predicted Summary to use the same wording as your inferred summary.
- Award full credit when the predicted Summary captures the same product, main issue/praise, relevant context, and objective tone.
- Do not penalize harmless inclusion of a secondary issue when it is explicitly stated and does not change the main meaning.
- Do penalize omission of a secondary issue only when that issue materially contributes to return, dissatisfaction, value concern, or final decision.

When ref_summary is NOT EMPTY:
- Continue to use ref_summary as the scoring target.
- Harmless paraphrasing should not reduce score.
- Penalize only material omissions, contradictions, exaggerations, or changes in primary meaning.

====================================================
PERSONALIZED MESSAGE INFERENCE RULES
====================================================
Infer the ideal PersonalizedMessage using these rules:
It should:
- include the company name exactly: "ChicStyle"
- reference the issue or praise explicitly
- sound natural and polite
Tone rules:
- Positive -> thank + appreciation
- Neutral -> acknowledge + light reassurance
- Negative -> apologize + clear next step such as support, exchange,
refund, or return help
Do not overpromise.
====================================================
PERSONALIZED MESSAGE SCORING
====================================================
REFERENCE OVERRIDE:
- If ref_personalized_message is NOT EMPTY:
 -> treat ref_personalized_message as the scoring target and inferred message
 -> evaluate predicted message against the reference message by semantic equivalence
 -> do NOT require exact wording
Evaluate ONLY the literal predicted message.
Score by components:
1. Brand inclusion
- includes "ChicStyle" exactly -> 0.2
- otherwise -> 0.0
2. Issue/Praise relevance
- directly references the scoring target's core praise or problem -> 0.3
- generic but directionally appropriate -> 0.15
- not relevant or contradicts target -> 0.0
3. Tone appropriateness
- matches the scoring target and inferred/reference sentiment well -> 0.2
- partially appropriate -> 0.1
- inappropriate -> 0.0
4. Actionability when needed
- For Negative: includes apology + a concrete next step/resolution path -> 0.3
- For Neutral: includes acknowledgement; next step/reassurance is helpful but not mandatory -> up to 0.3
- For Positive: thanks/appreciation is sufficient -> up to 0.3
Exact-match / semantic-equivalence shortcut:
- If predicted message is materially equivalent to the scoring target and satisfies all rules -> score 1.0
Important:
- Do NOT give credit for missing elements that are only implied
- Do NOT use content from other predicted fields
- Do NOT penalize harmless paraphrasing when reference message is provided
- Excessively generic wording should reduce relevance/actionability credit
PERSONALIZED MESSAGE REASON RULES
- Explain which message components are present or missing relative to the scoring target
- If score = 1.0, reason should indicate brand, relevance, tone, and actionability are all satisfied

====================================================
PERSONALIZED MESSAGE SEMANTIC SCORING CLARIFICATION (v6)
====================================================
The PersonalizedMessage score should reward explicit, relevant, customer-facing usefulness.

When ref_personalized_message is EMPTY:
- Infer the ideal message from the INPUT and sentiment.
- Do NOT require exact wording.
- Award full credit when the predicted message:
  1. includes "ChicStyle" exactly
  2. explicitly references the main issue or praise
  3. uses tone appropriate to inferred sentiment
  4. includes a concrete next step when sentiment is Negative

When ref_personalized_message is NOT EMPTY:
- Continue to use ref_personalized_message as the scoring target.
- Award full credit for semantic equivalence even if wording differs.
- Do not penalize harmless paraphrasing.
- Penalize only if the predicted message loses brand mention, issue/praise relevance, tone, or needed action.

Message specificity:
- Generic wording should reduce Issue/Praise relevance unless the specific issue or praise is explicitly identifiable in the predicted message.
- Do not infer missing specificity from Category, Summary, or other fields.

====================================================
RETAIL INSIGHT URGENCY INFERENCE RULES
====================================================
Allowed labels:
["High", "Medium", "Low"]
Infer Urgency using BUSINESS IMPACT, not just sentiment.
HIGH:
- defective or damaged product
- wrong item received
- severe fit issue / unusable product
- strong dissatisfaction or frustration
- explicit return or refund intent
- multiple serious complaints in one review
MEDIUM:
- delivery delay
- noticeable but not critical issue
- mixed sentiment with dissatisfaction
- repeated inconvenience or moderate dissatisfaction
LOW:
- positive feedback
- minor concerns or suggestions
- no actionable issue
- neutral review without meaningful complaint
Escalation rules:
1. Negative + product unusable -> High
2. Negative + inconvenience only -> Medium
3. Neutral + issue present -> Medium
4. Positive -> Low
Priority of evidence:
1. Review Text
2. Rating
3. Recommended
====================================================
RETAIL INSIGHT URGENCY SCORING
====================================================
REFERENCE OVERRIDE:
- If ref_urgency is NOT EMPTY:
 -> treat ref_urgency as inferred urgency
Validity:
Predicted Urgency is valid if and only if it EXACTLY matches one of:
High, Medium, Low
Scoring:
- If invalid -> 0.0
- If exact match to inferred -> 1.0
- Otherwise -> 0.0
REASON RULES
- If score = 1.0 -> reason MUST indicate correct urgency match
- If invalid -> reason MUST indicate invalid label and allowed set
- If incorrect -> reason MUST indicate expected inferred urgency
- Do NOT mix validity and correctness
====================================================
FINAL SCORE
====================================================
Compute:
final_score = average of
- Category
- Sentiment
- Summary
- PersonalizedMessage
- RetailInsight
Clamp final_score to [0.0, 1.0].

====================================================
REASON / SCORE CONSISTENCY RULE (v6)
====================================================
Before final output, verify that each score and reason agree.

Mandatory consistency checks:
- If a reason says the predicted value is correct, matches, or is equivalent to the inferred/reference value -> the score for that field MUST be 1.0.
- If a score is 1.0 -> the reason MUST indicate exact match or full semantic equivalence.
- If a score is 0.5 -> the reason MUST explain partial credit and identify the preferred target.
- If a score is 0.0 -> the reason MUST clearly state the expected target and why the predicted value is unsupported, invalid, contradictory, or materially incomplete.
- Do NOT produce a reason that says a prediction matches while assigning 0.0.
- Do NOT produce a reason that says a prediction is incorrect while assigning 1.0.

====================================================
OUTPUT REQUIREMENTS
====================================================
Return STRICT JSON only.
Rules:
- Include inferred values
- Include score reasons
- No markdown
- No extra commentary
- No trailing commas
- No contradictions between score and reason
- All scores must be in [0.0, 1.0]
For the inferred object:
- "RetailInsight" should contain ONLY the inferred urgency label as a
string
- For fields with reference values, the "inferred" output MUST reflect the reference value
"""
),
(
"user",
"""
INPUT:
Clothing ID: {clothing_id}
Review Title: {title}
Review Text: {review}
Rating: {rating}
Recommended: {recommended}
Division: {division}
Department: {department}
ClassName: {class_name}
PREDICTED VALUES:
Category: {predicted_category}
Sentiment: {predicted_sentiment}
Summary: {predicted_summary}
PersonalizedMessage: <<<{predicted_personalized_message}>>>
RetailInsightUrgency: {predicted_retail_insight_urgency}
REFERENCE VALUES:
Reference Category: {ref_category}
Reference Sentiment: {ref_sentiment}
Reference Summary: {ref_summary}
Reference PersonalizedMessage: {ref_personalized_message}
Reference Urgency: {ref_urgency}

Return JSON in this exact format:
{{
 "scores": {{
 "Category": 0.0,
 "Sentiment": 0.0,
 "Summary": 0.0,
 "PersonalizedMessage": 0.0,
 "RetailInsight": 0.0
 }},
 "final_score": 0.0,
 "inferred": {{
 "Category": "",
 "Sentiment": "",
 "Summary": "",
 "PersonalizedMessage": "",
 "RetailInsight": ""
 }},
 "score_reasons": {{
 "Category": "",
 "Sentiment": "",
 "Summary": "",
 "PersonalizedMessage": "",
 "RetailInsight": ""
 }}
}}
"""
)
])

Summary of the LLM_JUDGE_PROMPT improvement from milestone submission

Final Evaluation Clarifications

Apply these clarifications while preserving the original scoring schema.

Field Isolation
  • Evaluate each predicted field independently.
  • Do not use evidence from one predicted field to give credit to another field.
  • If a detail is absent from the predicted field being scored, treat it as absent.
Summary Evaluation Clarifications

A high-quality Summary should:

  • Clearly reference the product type when available
  • Capture the dominant issue or praise
  • Include the correct context (e.g., fit, quality, delivery, price, etc.)
  • Remain factual, concise, and non-exaggerated

Penalize Summary if it:

  • Focuses on a secondary issue while missing the dominant issue
  • Uses vague product references when a product type is available
  • Exaggerates severity beyond the review text
  • Repeats the same idea unnecessarily
  • Ignores an important positive detail in a clearly mixed review when that omission makes the summary misleading

Secondary issues may receive credit only if they are explicitly stated and do not overshadow the main issue.

Personalized Message Evaluation Clarifications

A high-quality PersonalizedMessage should:

  • Include "ChicStyle" exactly
  • Reference the specific issue or praise in the review
  • Match the inferred sentiment tone
  • Include a concrete next step for negative reviews

Penalize messages that are generic, even if polite.
For negative reviews, an apology without a clear next step should not receive full actionability credit.

Assumption Control
  • Do not reward unsupported assumptions.
  • Do not infer missing details.
  • Do not give credit for details that are only implied by the prompt template but not present in the predicted output.

Main logic implementation

Review Processing With Agents
  • Focus is LLM model analysis itself. But engineering matters.
  • With the goal leading to an enterprise-grade solution, we adopt the state-of-art industrial design and define agents for the workflow pipeline.
  • Besides formatlity, the approach sets up a framework for easy expansion and extension.
In [25]:
@dataclass
class RAGRetrievalAgent:
    """
    Retrieves similar review examples / ground-truth references from a vector store.
    If no vector store is supplied, it returns empty references.
    """
    vector_store: Chroma = None
    num_results: int = 3
    max_distance: Optional[float] = 0.05

    @staticmethod
    def safe_int(x):
        try:
            return int(x)
        except (TypeError, ValueError):
            return None

    def run(self, review_input: Any,  # JSON string or dict
            ) -> Dict[str, Any]:

        # Parse input
        if isinstance(review_input, str):
            try:
                review_input = json.loads(review_input)
            except json.JSONDecodeError:
                raise ValueError("Invalid JSON string input")

        if not isinstance(review_input, dict):
            raise ValueError("Input must be a dict or JSON string")

        title = review_input.get("Title", "")
        review = review_input.get("Review", "")
        rating = review_input.get("Rating", None)
        recommendation = review_input.get("Recommendation", None)

        # Build query (ONLY Title + Review)
        query_text = (
            f"{title}\n"
            f"{review}" # exactly the same in build_review_ground_truth_chroma()
        )
        # print(f"Index: {review_input.get("original_index", "")}\nQuery: {query_text}")
        # Retrieve (get extra candidates for filtering)
        results = self.vector_store.similarity_search_with_score(
            query=query_text,
            k = self.num_results + 5,
        )

        if not results:
            return {
                "Category": "",
                "Summary": "",
                "PersonalizedMessage": "",
                "Sentiment": "",
                "Urgency": "",
                "matched": False,
                "reason": "No results from vector search",
            }

        # Apply BOTH filters:
        # 1. distance threshold
        # 2. metadata match (Rating + Recommendation)
        valid_results = []
        for doc, dist in results:
            metadata = doc.metadata
            # Distance filter
            if dist > self.max_distance:
                continue
            # Metadata filter (strict match)
            meta_rating = RAGRetrievalAgent.safe_int(metadata.get("Rating"))
            meta_reco = RAGRetrievalAgent.safe_int(metadata.get("Recommendation"))
            input_rating = RAGRetrievalAgent.safe_int(rating)
            input_reco = RAGRetrievalAgent.safe_int(recommendation)
            if rating is not None and meta_rating != input_rating:
                continue
            if recommendation is not None and meta_reco != input_reco:
                continue
            valid_results.append((doc, dist))

        if not valid_results:
            return {
                "Category": "",
                "Summary": "",
                "PersonalizedMessage": "",
                "Sentiment": "",
                "Urgency": "",
                "matched": False,
                "reason": "No match after applying distance + metadata filters",
            }

        # Best match
        best_doc, distance = valid_results[0]
        # Pick result with smallest distance
        best_doc, distance = min(valid_results, key=lambda x: x[1])
        metadata = best_doc.metadata

        # Return JSON
        return {
            "Category": metadata.get("Category", ""),
            "Summary": metadata.get("Summary", ""),
            "PersonalizedMessage": metadata.get("PersonalizedMessage", ""),
            "Sentiment": metadata.get("Sentiment", ""),
            "Urgency": metadata.get("Urgency", ""),
            "matched": True,
            "similarity_distance": float(distance),
            "original_index": metadata.get("original_index"),
        }

class BaseLLMAgent:
    """
    Base agent for prompt -> LLM -> parser execution.

    Responsibilities:
    - Own a single task role, such as review analysis, RAG-grounded generation, or comparison
    - Convert dataframe rows into prompt inputs
    - Apply rate limiting
    - Return structured outputs with success/error metadata
    """

    def __init__(self, llm, parser, input_column_map, input_default_map=None,
                 agent_name="Base LLM Agent", calls=5, period=1, default_prompt=None):
        self.llm = llm
        self.parser = parser
        self.calls = calls
        self.period = period
        self.input_column_map = input_column_map
        self.input_default_map = input_default_map or {}
        self.agent_name = agent_name
        self.default_prompt = default_prompt

    def _resolve_prompt(self, prompt=None):
        resolved_prompt = prompt or self.default_prompt
        if resolved_prompt is None:
            raise ValueError(f"{self.agent_name} requires a prompt.")
        return resolved_prompt

    def _build_chain(self, prompt=None):
        return self._resolve_prompt(prompt) | self.llm | self.parser

    def _rate_limited_invoke(self, inputs, prompt=None):
        chain = self._build_chain(prompt)

        @sleep_and_retry
        @limits(calls=self.calls, period=self.period)
        def call():
            return chain.invoke(inputs)

        return call()

    def prepare_inputs(self, df):
        return Utils.df_to_langchain_inputs(
            df,
            column_map=self.input_column_map,
            default_map=self.input_default_map
        )

    def process_one(self, inputs, prompt=None):
        try:
            result = self._rate_limited_invoke(inputs, prompt=prompt)
            return {
                "success": True,
                "agent": self.agent_name,
                "result": result,
            }
        except Exception as e:
            return {
                "success": False,
                "agent": self.agent_name,
                "error": str(e),
            }

    def process_df(self, df, prompt=None):
        inputs_list = self.prepare_inputs(df)
        results = [self.process_one(inputs, prompt=prompt) for inputs in inputs_list]
        return Utils.results_to_dataframe(results, df.index)

    def process_df_with_progress(self, df, prompt=None, sleep_between=0, show_progress=False):
        inputs_list = self.prepare_inputs(df)

        if show_progress:
            try:
                from tqdm.notebook import tqdm
            except ImportError:
                from tqdm import tqdm
            iterator = tqdm(inputs_list, desc=self.agent_name)
        else:
            iterator = inputs_list

        results = []
        success_count = 0

        for inputs in iterator:
            res = self.process_one(inputs, prompt=prompt)
            results.append(res)

            if res["success"]:
                success_count += 1

            if show_progress:
                iterator.set_postfix(success=success_count)

            if sleep_between > 0:
                time.sleep(sleep_between)

        result = Utils.results_to_dataframe(results, df.index)
        return pd.concat([df, result], axis=1)

class ReviewAnalysisAgent(BaseLLMAgent):
    """
    Agent responsible for the core review-intelligence task:
    Category, Sentiment, Summary, PersonalizedMessage, and Urgency.
    """

    def __init__(self, *args, **kwargs):
        kwargs.setdefault("agent_name", "Review Analysis Agent")
        super().__init__(*args, **kwargs)


class RAGGroundedReviewAgent(ReviewAnalysisAgent):
    """
    Agent responsible for RAG-grounded review analysis.
    It expects reference fields in the input dataframe and uses them as grounding context.
    """

    def __init__(self, *args, **kwargs):
        kwargs.setdefault("agent_name", "RAG-Grounded Review Analysis Agent")
        super().__init__(*args, **kwargs)


class RecommendationPredictionAgent(BaseLLMAgent):
    """Agent for predicting customer recommendation intent from review text."""

    def __init__(self, *args, **kwargs):
        kwargs.setdefault("agent_name", "Recommendation Prediction Agent")
        super().__init__(*args, **kwargs)


class MismatchReasoningAgent(BaseLLMAgent):
    """Agent for comparing model/prompt outputs or explaining mismatches."""

    def __init__(self, *args, **kwargs):
        kwargs.setdefault("agent_name", "Mismatch Reasoning Agent")
        super().__init__(*args, **kwargs)
Instantiate ReviewAnalysisAgent and LlmJudge instances using LangChian LLM model
In [26]:
# key-value map to map df columns to keys in the prompt for review processing prompt
review_input_column_map = {
    "ClothingID": "clothing_id",
    "Title": "title",
    "Review": "review",
    "Rating": "rating",
    "Recommended": "recommended",
    "Division": "division",
    "Department": "department",
    "ClassName": "class_name",
    "original_index": "record_id" # used for record identificaiton
}
# default values in case the asscioated key does not have value for a column
review_input_default_map = {
    "title": "",
    "review": "[No comment]",
}
# create ReviewAnalysisAgent instance and set rate limit.
parser = JsonOutputParser()
review_analysis_agent = ReviewAnalysisAgent(
    llm, parser, review_input_column_map, review_input_default_map,
    agent_name="CoT Review Analysis Agent", calls=1, period=3
)
result_cols_to_show = ['original_index', 'Title','Review', "Category", "Sentiment", "Summary", "PersonalizedMessage", "RetailInsight.Urgency"]

# create LlmJudge instance and set rate limit.
review_input_columns = list(review_input_column_map.keys())
prediction_column_map = {
    "Category": "predicted_category",
    "Sentiment": "predicted_sentiment",
    "Summary": "predicted_summary",
    "PersonalizedMessage": "predicted_personalized_message",
    "RetailInsight.Urgency": "predicted_retail_insight_urgency"
}
prediction_columns= list(prediction_column_map.keys())

reference_column_map = {
    "RefCategory": "ref_category",
    "RefSentiment": "ref_sentiment",
    "RefSummary": "ref_summary",
    "RefPersonalizedMessage": "ref_personalized_message",
    "RefUrgency": "ref_urgency"
}
reference_columns = list(reference_column_map.keys())
llm_judge = LlmJudge(LLM_JUDGE_PROMPT, input_cols = review_input_columns,
                     prediction_cols = prediction_columns,
                     input_key_map = review_input_column_map,
                     prediction_key_map = prediction_column_map,
                     reference_cols = reference_columns,
                     reference_key_map = reference_column_map,
                     model_name=model_name,
                     api_key=api_key, base_url=base_url, calls=1, period=5)

VALID_CATEGORIES = {"Fit", "Quality", "Delivery", "Price", "Other"}

# wrapper for evaluation
def evaluate_prediction(df_result, repeat=3):
    scores, result = [], []
    for _ in range(repeat): # repeat to reduce variance
      evaluation = llm_judge.evaluate_batch(df_result, show_progress=True)
      result.append(evaluation)
      scores.append(evaluation['average_score'])
    print(f"scores: {scores}")
    print(f"average score: {np.average(scores)}")
    return result

Sample dataset with representative records for all ratings

In [27]:
# weighted random sample with fixed sample size
def weighted_stratified_sample(df, col, n, weights, random_state=42):
    rng = np.random.default_rng(random_state)

    # ---- preserve original index ----
    df = df.copy()
    df["original_index"] = df.index

    group_sizes = df.groupby(col).size()

    # Step 1: compute weighted scores
    weighted_scores = {}
    total = 0

    for g, size in group_sizes.items():
        w = weights.get(g, 1.0)
        score = size * w
        weighted_scores[g] = score
        total += score

    # Step 2: ideal allocations (float)
    raw_alloc = {
        g: (score / total) * n
        for g, score in weighted_scores.items()
    }

    # Step 3: floor allocations
    alloc = {g: int(np.floor(v)) for g, v in raw_alloc.items()}

    # Step 4: distribute remaining samples
    remaining = n - sum(alloc.values())

    if remaining > 0:
        remainders = {
            g: raw_alloc[g] - alloc[g]
            for g in raw_alloc
        }

        sorted_groups = sorted(remainders.items(), key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True)

        for i in range(remaining):
            g = sorted_groups[i % len(sorted_groups)][0]
            alloc[g] += 1

    # Step 5: sample per group
    samples = []

    for g, k in alloc.items():
        group_df = df[df[col] == g]

        k = min(k, len(group_df))  # safety cap

        samples.append(
            group_df.sample(n=k, random_state=random_state)
        )

    result = pd.concat(samples)

    # Final shuffle (index reset but original preserved)
    return result.sample(frac=1, random_state=random_state).reset_index(drop=True)
In [28]:
# Give low rating reviews more sampling weight since there are much less low-rating reviews than high-rating reviews.
df_sampled = weighted_stratified_sample(df_has_review, col="Rating", n=100,
    weights={
        1: 5,     # oversample
        2: 4,
        3: 3,
        4: 2,
        5: 1      # undersample
    }
)
# Save the sample as csv file to be used later for consistency as the sample is random.
# path to saved sample
path_df_sampled = path.join(project_root,'GenAIReviewCategorization','df_sampled.tsv')
df_sampled.to_csv(path_df_sampled, index=False, sep="\t", quoting=csv.QUOTE_MINIMAL)
# load the df_sampled
# df_sampled = pd.read_csv(path_df_sampled, sep="\t")

Evaluation Result of prompting techniques - Findings from milestone submission

In [30]:
from collections import Counter

#Helper method to plot side-by-side histograms for evaluation scores
def plot_evaluation_comparison_histograms(evaluations_list, legend=None, prompt_technique='', bar_width=0.03):

    keys = ['Category', 'PersonalizedMessage', 'Summary', 'Sentiment', 'RetailInsight']
    n_versions = len(evaluations_list)
    # Default legend
    if legend is None:
        legend = [f'v{i+1}' for i in range(n_versions)]

    fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 5, figsize=(12, 3))
    axes = axes.flatten()
    for i, key in enumerate(keys):
        # Collect values for each version
        values_list = []
        counters = []
        for eval_data in evaluations_list:
            values = [round(x['scores'][key], 2) for x in eval_data[0]['details']]
            values_list.append(values)
            counters.append(Counter(values))

        # Combine all score points
        score_points = sorted(set().union(*values_list))

        # Dynamic bar width based on number of versions
        bar_width = min(bar_width, 0.8 / (len(score_points) * n_versions))

        # Plot each version
        for j in range(n_versions):
            counts = [counters[j].get(s, 0) for s in score_points]
            # Offset bars for side-by-side effect
            x_positions = [
                s + (j - (n_versions - 1) / 2) * bar_width
                for s in score_points
            ]

            bars = axes[i].bar(
                x_positions,
                counts,
                width=bar_width,
                label=legend[j]
            )

            # Add value labels
            for bar in bars:
                height = bar.get_height()
                if height > 0:
                    axes[i].text(
                        bar.get_x() + bar.get_width() / 2, height, f'{int(height)}',
                        ha='center', va='bottom', fontsize=5, rotation=90)

        # Formatting
        ticks = np.round(np.arange(0, 1.05, 0.05), 2)
        axes[i].set_xticks(ticks)
        axes[i].set_title(key, fontsize=9)
        axes[i].set_xlabel(f"{prompt_technique} score", fontsize=7)
        axes[i].set_ylabel("Frequency", fontsize=5)
        labels = [f"{t:.2f}" if (round(t*100) % 10 == 0) else "" for t in ticks]
        axes[i].set_xticklabels(labels, fontsize=6)
        axes[i].legend(fontsize=7)
        axes[i].tick_params(axis='x', rotation=45)
        axes[i].tick_params(axis='y', labelsize=5)

    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()
Zero-Shot prompt V1 & V2 score comparision
In [31]:
# Load saved zero_shot_result_v1 dataframe and zero_shot_v1_evaluations from milestone submission
path_zero_shot_result_v1 = path.join(project_root, project_folder,'zero_shot_result_v1.tsv')
df_zero_shot_result_v1 = pd.read_csv(path_zero_shot_result_v1, sep="\t")
path_zero_shot_v1_evaluations = path.join(project_root, project_folder,'zero_shot_v1_evaluations.json')
with open(path_zero_shot_v1_evaluations, "r") as f:
    zero_shot_v1_evaluations = json.load(f)

# Load saved zero_shot_result_v2 dataframe and zero_shot_v2_evaluations from milestone submission
path_zero_shot_result_v2 = path.join(project_root, project_folder,'zero_shot_result_v2.tsv')
df_zero_shot_result_v2 = pd.read_csv(path_zero_shot_result_v2, sep="\t")
path_zero_shot_v2_evaluations = path.join(project_root, project_folder,'zero_shot_v2_evaluations.json')
with open(path_zero_shot_v2_evaluations, "r") as f:
    zero_shot_v2_evaluations = json.load(f)
# Comparison of scores zero-shot  V1 vs V2
plot_evaluation_comparison_histograms([zero_shot_v1_evaluations, zero_shot_v2_evaluations], prompt_technique='zero-shot')
