In [60]:






# Comparison of CoT vs RAG+CoT
plot_evaluation_comparison_histograms([CoT_evaluations, RAG_CoT_evaluations], legend=['CoT','RAG+CoT'], bar_width=0.03)
















































Overall Performance Comparison by LLM Judge
LLM-judge evaluation score


| Model            | V1     | V2     |
|----------------- |--------|--------|
| RAG + CoT        |   x    | 0.979  |
| Chain-of-Thought | 0.784  | 0.932  |
| Few-shot         | 0.780  | 0.929  |
| Zero-shot        | 0.772  | 0.906  |


















In [61]:






scores_RAG_CoT_evaluations = [round(x['final_score'], 3) for x in RAG_CoT_evaluations[0]['details']]
scores_CoT_v2_evaluations = [round(x['final_score'], 3) for x in CoT_v2_evaluations[0]['details']]
scores_zero_shot_v2_evaluations = [round(x['final_score'], 3) for x in zero_shot_v2_evaluations[0]['details']]
scores_few_shot_v2_evaluations = [round(x['final_score'], 3) for x in few_shot_v2_evaluations[0]['details']]
t_stat_1, p_val_1_two_sided = stats.ttest_rel(scores_zero_shot_v2_evaluations, scores_few_shot_v2_evaluations)
# Convert to one-sided p-value
p_val_1 = p_val_1_two_sided / 2 if t_stat_1 < 0 else 1 - (p_val_1_two_sided / 2)
print(f"Test 1: ZeroShot < FewShot\nt-stat: {t_stat_1} p-value:{p_val_1}")

t_stat_2, p_val_2_two_sided = stats.ttest_rel(scores_few_shot_v2_evaluations, scores_CoT_v2_evaluations)
p_val_2 = p_val_2_two_sided / 2 if t_stat_2 < 0 else 1 - (p_val_2_two_sided / 2)
print(f"\nTest 2: FewShot < CoT\nt-stat: {t_stat_2} p-value:{p_val_2}")

t_stat_3, p_val_3_two_sided = stats.ttest_rel(scores_CoT_v2_evaluations, scores_RAG_CoT_evaluations)
p_val_3 = p_val_3_two_sided / 2 if t_stat_2 < 0 else 1 - (p_val_3_two_sided / 2)
print(f"\nTest 3: CoT < RAG+CoT\nt-stat: {t_stat_3} p-value:{p_val_3}")

























Test 1: ZeroShot < FewShot
t-stat: -2.651572439766495 p-value:0.004665423895222856

Test 2: FewShot < CoT
t-stat: -0.2451719592805966 p-value:0.40341539206104826

Test 3: CoT < RAG+CoT
t-stat: -3.279811006518187 p-value:0.0007172557616758937

























Observation
    

  • Few-shot is significantly better than zero-shot
    • 

  • CoT is moderately better than few-shot.
    • 

  • RAG+CoT is significantly bettern than CoT
    • 























Insights
When no reference is provided, the LLM judge compares predictions against internally inferred values based on the judge prompt. A prediction prompt that is structurally closer to the judge’s inference logic will likely score higher. Thus, a reference-free LLM judge is more suitable for pairwise comparison between different versions of the same technique. To establish an absolute baseline across different techniques, a ground truth dataset is a better approach. However, a ground truth approach is not scalable with a large number of reviews unless there is an existing historical response dataset that can serve as ground truth.


    

  • How do the techniques (Zero-Shot, Few-Shot, CoT, RAG+CoT) compare in terms of their responses? Use LLM to give verdict?
    

      

    • For each technique, V2 is usually better than V1, which is expected due to more structured rules.
      • 

    • Overall performance: RAG + CoT > CoT > Few-Shot > Zero-Shot.
      • 

    

    • 

  • Which technique was the most reliable and consistent? Why do you think it performed the best?
    

    Out of all techniques, RAG + CoT is the best, followed by CoT. CoT forces intermediate structure. Without CoT, models often jump straight to an answer. That increases:
    

      

    • shortcut reasoning
      • 

    • pattern matching
      • 

    • hidden assumption errors
      • 

    

    CoT breaks this into steps like:
    

    infer → compare → decide → explain
    

    RAG further improves performance by adding grounding from retrieved reference data. This:
    

      

    • reduces hallucination
      • 

    • improves consistency across similar inputs
      • 

    • aligns outputs with expected patterns used in evaluation
      • 

    

    The combination of RAG (grounding) + CoT (structured reasoning) leads to the most reliable and consistent results.
    

    • 

  • What model and prompt design would you propose for a production environment?
    

      

    • Based on the above experiment and analysis, RAG + CoT is recommended for a production environment.
      • 

    • Proposed design:
        

      • Use a strong LLM with Chain-of-Thought structured prompting
        • 

      • Add a retrieval layer (RAG) using historical labeled reviews or responses
        • 

      • Pipeline:
          

        • Input → Retrieve (RAG) → CoT reasoning → Structured Output
          • 

        

        • 

      

      • 

    • This approach provides:
        

      • higher accuracy
        • 

      • better consistency
        • 

      • stronger alignment with business rules
        • 

      • scalability when reference data is available
        • 

      

      • 

    

    • 























Applying GenAI for Product Recommendation:
Now, let's use the model for a different task: predicting the Recommended flag.


Questions:


    

  1. How do you design a prompt that strictly asks for a binary output (1 or 0) and a brief reason?
    

    2. 

  2. What kind of function is needed to reliably parse the model's text response to extract the 1/0 flag and the Reason?
    

    3. 

  3. How do you evaluate the model's performance as a classifier using standard metrics like accuracy, confusion matrix, and classification report?
    

    4. 























Recommendation Prediction based on Review only


















In [62]:






# create a strictly two-line prompt
recommendation_prediction_prompt = ChatPromptTemplate.from_template("""Based ONLY on this review text: {review}, output a binary recommendation (1 = recommend, 0 = not recommend).
Return STRICT JSON: {{"PredictedRecommendation": 1 or 0, "Reason": "<short reason under 15 words>"}}; no extra text.""")





















In [63]:






review_only_input_column_map = {
    "Review": "review"
}
review_only_input_default_map = {
    "review": "[No comment]" # defalut value if no review
}
# create RecommendationPredictionAgent instance and set rate limit.
parser = JsonOutputParser()
recommendation_prediction_agent = RecommendationPredictionAgent(llm, parser, review_only_input_column_map, review_only_input_default_map, calls=1, period=3)





















In [64]:






with ProcessTimer("Predicting recommendation based on review text only"):
    df_recommendation_predicted = recommendation_prediction_agent.process_df_with_progress(df_sampled, recommendation_prediction_prompt, show_progress=True)

























Recommendation Prediction Agent:   0%|          | 0/100 [00:00<?, ?it/s]












Predicting recommendation based on review text only took 69.503139 seconds

























Mismatched prediction and reasons for mismatch


















In [65]:






# convert boolean to 1/0 integer value
df_recommendation_predicted["Recommendation"] = df_recommendation_predicted["Recommended"].fillna(False).astype(int)
cols_to_show = ["original_index","Title","Review","Rating","Recommendation","PredictedRecommendation"]
# show rows where predicted value not equal to actual value for Recommendation
df_recommendation_mismatch = df_recommendation_predicted[df_recommendation_predicted.PredictedRecommendation!=df_recommendation_predicted.Recommendation][cols_to_show]
df_recommendation_mismatch.style.hide(axis="index")

0




























Observation


    

  • There is only one case where the incorrect prediction is 1, the associated review (index=4107) reads quite positive but with a rating of only 2. The review itself thus can be misleading for LLM without rating as extra input.
    • 

  • For cases where the incorrect predictions are 0, the reviews reads with dominantly negative sentiment, causing LLM to think there should be no recommendation. This can be improved by adding rating as an extra input.
    • 























LLM explanation for mismatch


















In [66]:






recommendation_mismatch_reason_prompt = ChatPromptTemplate.from_template(
"""
You are an expert in analyzing discrepancies between human labels and model predictions in e-commerce review data.

Your task is to explain why a model’s PredictedRecommendation may differ from the human-labeled Recommendation using provided Title, Review text and Rating.

Be objective, precise, and evidence-based.

Do NOT speculate beyond the given input.
Do NOT assume the model or human is always correct.
Human Recommendation considers Title, Review text, and Rating.
Predicted Recommendation only considers Review text.
Focus on identifying signals in the review that justify each side.

Keep explanations concise and grounded in the text.

INPUT:
Title: {title}
Review: {review_text}
Rating: {rating}

Human Recommendation:
{human_recommendation}   # 1 = recommend, 0 = not recommend

Predicted Recommendation:
{predicted_recommendation}   # 1 = recommend, 0 = not recommend

====================================================
TASK
====================================================

Step 1: Identify key signals from the review
- Extract main positive aspects
- Extract main negative aspects
- Determine overall sentiment strength

Step 2: Interpret Human Recommendation
- Explain what signals likely led to the human decision
- Consider both review text and rating

Step 3: Interpret Predicted Recommendation
- Explain what signals the model likely relied on
- Identify if it focused on positive, negative, or mixed signals

Step 4: Explain the difference (CRITICAL)
- Why do the human and model disagree (if they do)?
- Is the review mixed, ambiguous, or imbalanced?
- Did one side overweight certain signals?

Step 5: Final judgment
- Which recommendation is better supported by the review?
- Or state if both are reasonable due to mixed signals

====================================================
OUTPUT FORMAT (STRICT JSON ONLY)
====================================================

{{
  "KeySignals": {{
    "Positive": ["..."],
    "Negative": ["..."]
  }},
  "HumanReasoning": "...",
  "ModelReasoning": "...",
  "DiscrepancyExplanation": "...",
  "BetterSupported": "Human | Model | Both",
  "Confidence": "High | Medium | Low"
}}
""")





















In [67]:






mismatch_reasoning_input_column_map = {
    "Title":"title",
    "Review": "review_text",
    "Rating": "rating",
    "Recommendation": "human_recommendation",
    "PredictedRecommendation": "predicted_recommendation"
}
mismatch_reasoning_input_default_map = {
    "title": "[No title]", # defalut value if no review
    "review_text": "[No comment]" # defalut value if no review
}
# create ReviewAnalysisAgent instance and set rate limit.
parser = JsonOutputParser()
mismatch_reasoning_agent = MismatchReasoningAgent(
    llm, parser, mismatch_reasoning_input_column_map,
    mismatch_reasoning_input_default_map, calls=1, period=3)





















In [68]:






with ProcessTimer("LLM comparison of Recommendation prediction"):
    df_recommendation_mismatch_reason = mismatch_reasoning_agent.process_df_with_progress(
        df_recommendation_mismatch, recommendation_mismatch_reason_prompt, show_progress=True)

























Mismatch Reasoning Agent:   0%|          | 0/13 [00:00<?, ?it/s]












LLM comparison of Recommendation prediction took 36.45969 seconds





















In [69]:






df_recommendation_mismatch_reason[["Review","HumanReasoning","ModelReasoning","DiscrepancyExplanation"]].style.hide(axis="index")





















Out[69]:









Review
HumanReasoning
ModelReasoning
DiscrepancyExplanation






I love this blouse, i've received so many compliments on it. unfortunately i just got it back from the dry cleaners and the color completely changed to a pale yellow! it also ate away some of the fabric, so now it's completely transparent... and i go to an organic cleaners! 

retailer: if you can fix the fabric quality this blouse would be a classic.
The human likely considered both the strong positive emotional attachment ('I love this blouse', 'so many compliments') and the significant negative experience with quality. The rating of 3 suggests a mixed but overall positive stance, and the human may have interpreted the positives as outweighing the negatives, or that the blouse is still recommendable with a warning about quality.
The model, using only the review text, likely focused on the severe negative signals about quality ('color completely changed', 'ate away some of the fabric', 'completely transparent'), which are strong indicators of a product defect. The positives are present but may be outweighed by the detailed description of product failure.
The disagreement arises because the human considered the rating and the initial enthusiasm in the title and text, balancing them against the negatives to still recommend the product. The model, restricted to the review text, likely gave more weight to the vivid negative details about product failure, leading to a non-recommendation. The review is mixed, but the negatives are severe and specific, while the positives are more general.




Loved this dress and ordered it based on glowing reviews. ordered size large because i weigh 155 pounds and have a large 34g bust. the dress skimmed nicely over my body but the baby doll style fit loosely and made me look really wide from the side. i probably could have sized down for a better fit, but it might have just emphasized my bust. the fabric has a substantial weight and it's lined. best as a fall, winter dress. the embroidery is beautiful. i disliked the intense black color around my f
The human likely considered both the positive aspects (love for the dress, quality fabric, beautiful embroidery) and the negatives (fit issues, color dislike), but judged that the positives outweighed the negatives enough to recommend, albeit with reservations. The moderate rating (3) suggests a mixed but overall positive impression.
The model, relying solely on the review text, likely focused on the negative signals: dissatisfaction with the fit, looking wide from the side, and dislike of the color. The positive comments may have been outweighed by these explicit negatives, leading to a non-recommendation.
The review is mixed, containing both strong positives and notable negatives. The human, with access to the rating and title, may have interpreted the overall sentiment as slightly positive, justifying a recommendation. The model, lacking access to the rating and title, likely weighted the negative comments about fit and color more heavily, resulting in a non-recommendation. The ambiguity and balance of signals in the review make both interpretations plausible.




I ordered xs petite and was pretty excited when this arrived based on the reviews. however (and maybe it's just me) the top was really, really large on me, especially around the shoulders and upper arms, so the shape was not flattering. sorry to have to return this item.
The human likely considered the high rating (4), initial excitement, and possibly interpreted the fit issue as specific to petites rather than a product flaw. The overall sentiment, while negative about fit, is softened by the positive rating and the possibility that the issue is not universal.
The model, relying only on the review text, focused on strong negative signals: the item was 'really, really large,' 'not flattering,' and 'had to return this item.' These outweigh the brief initial excitement, leading to a not recommend prediction.
The disagreement arises because the human factored in the high rating and possibly contextualized the negative comments as specific to the reviewer's body type, while the model only saw the negative textual signals about fit and return. The review is mixed: text is negative, but the rating is positive, creating ambiguity.




I was hoping this top would be a nice architectural piece to add to my wardrobe with its straight black line accents. sadly, in the end, it is just a t-shirt. a large t-shirt. i ordered my normal size (medium) and this shirt ran more like a large. i also did not like the way the neck was hemmed. the fabric folds over itself, when i think a better design would have been a more simple hem. i ended up returning this item due to its size.
The human likely considered the 4-star rating as a strong positive signal, indicating overall satisfaction despite the negative comments in the review text. The human may have interpreted the issues as minor or specific to personal preference, and thus still recommended the product.
The model, relying solely on the review text, identified multiple negative signals: disappointment with style, poor fit, dislike of the neck hem, and the fact that the item was returned. With no explicit positive statements, the model predicted 'not recommend.'
The discrepancy arises because the human factored in the high rating (4), which suggests general approval, while the model only saw the negative review text. The review text alone is strongly negative, but the rating contradicts this, creating ambiguity. The human may have weighted the rating more heavily, while the model ignored it.




I should have known with the combined sizing, but this is very wide, even in the xs/sm. it's a beautiful sweater, a thicker gauge of yarn than it appears to have, and the pointelle "holes" are also larger than they appear. it is fairly itchy if you don't mind that. just way too big for my small frame--almost falling of the shoulders, which is not the look i was going for. if you want a wide and boxy look, or have a larger frame, this could be great for you. it is pretty and quite unique.
The human likely considered both the positive descriptions of the sweater's appearance and uniqueness, as well as the overall 4-star rating, which indicates satisfaction despite fit and comfort issues. The reviewer also suggests the sweater could be great for others with different preferences or body types, supporting a recommendation.
The model, relying solely on the review text, likely focused on the repeated negative comments about fit ('way too big', 'almost falling off the shoulders'), comfort ('fairly itchy'), and the statement that it was 'not the look I was going for.' These negative signals may have outweighed the positive remarks, leading to a non-recommendation.
The disagreement arises because the human label incorporates the high rating and the nuanced suggestion that the product could be suitable for others, while the model only processes the review text and likely overweights the negative personal experience. The review is mixed, but the rating and broader applicability noted by the human are not accessible to the model.




I agree with the first reviewer. this dress is just ok; nothing to write home about. if you need a throw on dress in a pinch, it's nice to have on hand, but there are probably a lot of other retailer dresses that fit the bill that are better quality than this one. it is also very body conscious in the back. the material is a thin, ribbed rayon - on the cheap side. overall, it's a comfortable piece, but it's more like a thin, long-sleeve t-shirt, rather than a quality dress. it would have been a gr
The human likely considered the moderate 3-star rating, the mention of comfort and utility ('nice to have on hand', 'comfortable piece'), and judged that despite clear negatives, the dress is acceptable for certain uses. The overall tone is lukewarm but not entirely negative, supporting a cautious recommendation.
The model, relying solely on the review text, likely focused on the predominance of negative phrases describing quality, fit, and material, with only mild positives. The negative language outweighs the faint praise, leading to a non-recommendation.
The disagreement arises because the human factored in the 3-star rating and the faint positives, interpreting the review as mixed but ultimately passable for recommendation. The model, lacking access to the rating and title, weighted the negative textual signals more heavily, resulting in a non-recommendation. The review is mixed, but the negatives are more explicit in the text.




These look nothing like the picture! they are super high-waisted & longer than they look in this picture. i felt as if i was wearing a pair of jeans from the 90's that just fit terribly. i thought they were going to be a pair of cropped wide legs & they are far from it. the color was also much lighter than shown in picture. super unflattering & just not for me. they're going back...
The human labeler considered the strongly negative review text, the critical title ('Terrible cut'), and the low rating (2). Despite all negative signals, the human marked 'recommend', which may indicate a labeling error or misinterpretation, as there are no positive aspects present.
The model, using only the review text, identified exclusively negative language and sentiment, with no positive statements, leading it to predict 'not recommend'.
The discrepancy arises because the human label is 'recommend' despite the absence of any positive signals in the title, review, or rating. The model's prediction aligns with the overwhelmingly negative content. The review is not mixed or ambiguous; all signals are negative. The human likely made an error or misapplied the labeling criteria.




This top fit perfectly but too much of my bra was showing through the black lace. it would be a great top to use as loungewear, though.
The human likely considered both the negative experience (having to return the item due to excessive sheerness) and the low rating (2), which together indicate dissatisfaction and justify not recommending the product, despite some positive comments.
The model, relying only on the review text, likely focused on the positive phrases such as 'fit perfectly' and 'would be a great top to use as loungewear,' interpreting these as signals of satisfaction and thus predicting a recommendation, possibly underweighting the return and negative aspects.
The disagreement arises because the human incorporated the strong negative signal of returning the item and the low rating, while the model, limited to the review text, may have been influenced by the positive language and overlooked the significance of the return. The review is mixed, but the act of returning and the low rating are strong negative signals that the model could not access.




This is a cute dress, but just not a keeper for me. the top portion of the dress is fairly snug. i am normally a small in retailer sizes, but i would have sized up the keep the dress from pulling so much across the bust. otherwise, it was cute and simple, and the tulle was quite soft.

i think that i would have gone with a medium, but i am wearing a small in the photo. for reference, i am 5'5", ~135 lbs, 34c, and 27" waist.
The human likely considered the overall positive tone regarding the dress's appearance and fabric ('cute', 'simple', 'soft tulle'), as well as the moderate rating (3), and interpreted the sizing issue as a fixable problem rather than a dealbreaker. The explicit sizing advice and lack of strong negative language may have led the human to recommend the product, assuming others could size up for a better fit.
The model, relying solely on the review text, likely focused on the phrase 'just not a keeper for me' and the detailed fit issues ('snug', 'pulling across the bust'), interpreting these as strong negative signals that outweighed the positive descriptors. The model may have interpreted the personal decision not to keep the dress as a non-recommendation.
The disagreement arises because the review is mixed: it contains both positive comments about the dress and negative comments about fit. The human, with access to the rating and title, may have weighted the positives and the fixable nature of the negatives more heavily, while the model, limited to the review text, likely prioritized the explicit statement of not keeping the dress and the fit issues. The model may have underweighted the positive aspects due to the strong negative phrase 'not a keeper for me.'




I ordered the dress online and found it to be not as pictured.  the length hit several inches above the knee.  the sleeves are also 3/4 length rather than full length as pictured.  i found the cut to be much less fitted through the torso as well.  i tried both the small and xsmall, which are my usual sizes.  the small was very loose through the torso, the xsmall was better, but looked overall too short, as if it were a petite cut.  i am 5'9", however the model is supposed to be 5'10".
The human likely considered the 3-star rating, which suggests a neutral or slightly positive experience, and may have interpreted the review as informative rather than entirely negative. Despite the detailed complaints, the reviewer tried multiple sizes and did not explicitly state complete dissatisfaction or a refusal to recommend. The human may have weighed the moderate rating and the effort to find a fit as signals that the product could still be suitable for some, leading to a recommendation.
The model, relying solely on the review text, likely focused on the numerous negative signals: the dress not matching the picture, multiple fit issues, and dissatisfaction with sizing. The absence of any positive statements or explicit recommendation in the text likely led the model to predict 'not recommend.'
The discrepancy arises because the human considered both the 3-star rating and the review text, interpreting the moderate rating as a sign of partial satisfaction or acceptability. The model, limited to the review text, detected only negative feedback and no positive or mitigating statements, resulting in a 'not recommend' prediction. The review is imbalanced, with overwhelmingly negative textual signals but a neutral rating, leading to different interpretations.




I'm 5'3 135lbs with hour-glassy figure and the medium was a too tight from the waste down (but perfect on the top - i'm a 36c). pretty dress overall and looks true to color in photo, but have to return.
The human likely considered the overall positive comments about the dress's appearance, color accuracy, and fit on the top, as well as the high rating (4), indicating general satisfaction despite the sizing issue. The decision to recommend may reflect that the reviewer found the product good overall, with the main issue being fit, which could be resolved by sizing up.
The model, relying solely on the review text, likely focused on the negative signals: 'too tight from the waist down', 'have to return', and 'runs small'. The explicit statement about returning the item and the fit issue may have outweighed the positive remarks, leading to a prediction of not recommend.
The disagreement arises because the human incorporated the positive aspects and the high rating, interpreting the review as generally favorable despite the return. The model, lacking access to the rating and title, prioritized the negative action (returning the item) and fit complaint in the text, leading to a more negative assessment. The review is mixed, but the act of returning the product is a strong negative signal for the model.




I was excited to nab this lovely because i am a sucker for a faux layered look. i am usually an xs or s, depending on the designer so i went with xs in this top based on reviews that it doesn't drape well. i barely got my arms into the sleeves. i thought maybe it was a mistake and i received a child's size?  i eventually did get it on but barely got it back off. size up 1-2 for comfort if you have to have this girl. i was bummed my local retailer did not have a m for me to try. returned.  for ref:
The human likely considered the overall mixed tone: initial excitement and attraction to the style, but significant fit issues leading to a return. The rating of 3 suggests a neutral or slightly positive stance, possibly indicating that the reviewer still sees value in the product for others if they size up. The explicit advice to 'size up 1-2 for comfort if you have to have this girl' and the absence of strong negative language beyond fit issues may have led the human to recommend, albeit with reservations.
The model, relying solely on the review text, likely focused on the strong negative signals: severe fit problems, difficulty putting on and taking off the top, and the fact that the item was returned. The positive signals are brief and outweighed by the detailed negative experience, leading the model to predict 'not recommend.'
The disagreement arises because the human factored in the moderate rating (3) and the nuanced advice to size up, interpreting the review as a conditional recommendation. The model, lacking access to the rating and title, weighted the negative experience and return more heavily. The review is mixed but leans negative in the text, while the rating and advice introduce ambiguity.




The cotton, the print, and the online presentation of this dress are beautiful, i ordered right away! but there are some discrepancies. the shoulders have a "puff" to them that i don't notice on the model, and that doesn't flatter athletic shoulders. the buttons are (cheap) difficult to button. the waist sits much lower on the model than it must on anyone else, it's nearly empire on me. and, there is a lot more volume in the skirt of the dress than is shown in any photo. because the dress is tig
The human likely considered the initial strong positives about the dress's appearance and excitement to order, as well as the moderate rating (3), and judged that despite several fit and quality issues, the overall experience was still positive enough to recommend.
The model, relying only on the review text, likely focused on the detailed negative feedback about fit, quality, and discrepancies with the product, which outweigh the brief positive comments, leading to a non-recommendation.
The review contains both strong initial praise and a series of specific complaints. The human, with access to the rating and possibly weighing the initial enthusiasm, interpreted the overall sentiment as positive enough to recommend. The model, limited to the review text and seeing more detailed negatives, judged the sentiment as not recommend. The disagreement arises from the mixed nature of the review and the model's lack of access to the rating and title.




























Observation


    

  • LLM attributes mismatch most to mixed reviews and the fact that human labels include Rating and Title in addition to Review text.
    

      

    • Example:
      
Review: I was hoping this top would be a nice architectural piece to add to my wardrobe with its straight black line accents. sadly, in the end, it is just a t-shirt. a large t-shirt. i ordered my normal size (medium) and this shirt ran more like a large. i also did not like the way the neck was hemmed. the fabric folds over itself, when i think a better design would have been a more simple hem. i ended up returning this item due to its size.
      
Mismatch reason: The discrepancy arises because the human labeler incorporated the high rating (4), which typically signals satisfaction, while the model only had access to the review text, which is predominantly negative and concludes with a return. The review is mixed but leans negative in text, while the rating is positive, creating ambiguity. The human may have overweighted the rating, while the model overweighted the negative textual content.
      • 

    

    • 

  • This explanation is consistent with human explanation.
    

    • 



















In [70]:






from sklearn.metrics import classification_report

print(classification_report(df_recommendation_predicted["Recommendation"], df_recommendation_predicted["PredictedRecommendation"]))

























              precision    recall  f1-score   support

           0       0.76      0.97      0.86        40
           1       0.98      0.80      0.88        60

    accuracy                           0.87       100
   macro avg       0.87      0.89      0.87       100
weighted avg       0.89      0.87      0.87       100






















In [71]:






from sklearn.metrics import confusion_matrix

labels = sorted(df_recommendation_predicted["Recommendation"].unique())
cm = confusion_matrix(df_recommendation_predicted["Recommendation"], df_recommendation_predicted["PredictedRecommendation"], labels=labels)
plt.figure(figsize=(4,3))
sns.heatmap(cm, annot=True, fmt="d", xticklabels=labels, yticklabels=labels)
plt.xlabel("Predicted")
plt.ylabel("Actual")
plt.title("Confusion Matrix")
plt.show()
















































Visualization of Sentiments Distribution


After generating results from all prompting techniques, it's crucial to visualize their outputs to better understand their behavior and performance. This helps us see if one technique tends to be more cautious (e.g., assigning more 'Neutral' sentiments) or if they generally agree on the sentiment of the reviews.


Questions:


    

  • How does the distribution of predicted Sentiment (Positive, Negative, Neutral) compare across the V2 versions of Zero-Shot, Few-Shot, and Chain-of-Thought? (Hint: Create a separate bar chart for each technique's V2 sentiment column).
    

    • 

  • Are there noticeable differences in the counts? For example, does one technique identify more "Neutral" reviews than the others? What might this imply about its ability to handle nuance?
    

    • 



















In [72]:






# Histogram for predicted Sentiment: side-by-side V1 vs V2 for each prompt technique
sentiment_cols = {
    "zero_shot": {
        "v1": df_zero_shot_result_v1["Sentiment"], "v2": df_zero_shot_result_v2["Sentiment"],
    },
    "few_shot": {
        "v1": df_few_shot_result_v1["Sentiment"], "v2": df_few_shot_result_v2["Sentiment"],
    },
    "CoT": {
        "v1": df_CoT_result_v1["Sentiment"], "v2": df_CoT_result_v2["Sentiment"],
    },
}

order = ["Negative", "Neutral", "Positive"]
plot_df = pd.concat(
    [
        pd.DataFrame({
            "Technique": technique, "Version": version.upper(), "Sentiment": values
        })
        for technique, versions in sentiment_cols.items()
        for version, values in versions.items()
    ],
    ignore_index=True
)

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(8, 3), sharey=True)
axes = axes.flatten()

for i, technique in enumerate(["zero_shot", "few_shot", "CoT"]):
    ax = axes[i]

    sns.countplot(
        data=plot_df[plot_df["Technique"] == technique],
        x="Sentiment",
        hue="Version",
        order=order,
        ax=ax
    )

    ax.set_title(f"Sentiment distribution - {technique}", fontsize=9)
    ax.set_xlabel("")
    ax.set_ylabel("Count" if i == 0 else "", fontsize=8)
    ax.tick_params(axis="x", labelsize=8, rotation=45)
    ax.tick_params(axis="y", labelsize=8)
    ax.legend(title="", fontsize=8)

    # 🔹 Add value labels on top of bars
    for container in ax.containers:
        ax.bar_label(container, fmt='%d', fontsize=7, padding=2)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()












































In [73]:






# We now compare how three techniques are different in sentiment prediction.
# Step 1: Rename columns to avoid collisions
df1 = df_zero_shot_result_v2.rename(columns={
    #"Category": "Category_zs",
    "Sentiment": "Sentiment_zs"
})

df2 = df_few_shot_result_v2.rename(columns={
    #"Category": "Category_fs",
    "Sentiment": "Sentiment_fs"
})

df3 = df_CoT_result_v2.rename(columns={
    #"Category": "Category_cot",
    "Sentiment": "Sentiment_cot"
})

# Step 2: Merge dataframes on original_index
df_merged = df1.merge(
    df2[["original_index", "Sentiment_fs"]],
    on="original_index"
).merge(
    df3[["original_index", "Sentiment_cot"]],
    on="original_index"
)

# Step 3: Find rows where Sentiment differ across models
mask_diff = df_merged[["Sentiment_zs", "Sentiment_fs", "Sentiment_cot"]].nunique(axis=1) > 1

df_diff = df_merged[mask_diff]

# Step 4: Select final columns for inspection
result = df_diff[[
    "original_index",
    "Title",
    "Review",
    "Rating",
    #"Category_zs", "Category_fs", "Category_cot",
    "Sentiment_zs", "Sentiment_fs", "Sentiment_cot"
]]

# Optional: reset index for clean display
result = result.reset_index(drop=True)

# Optional: number of disagreements
print(f"Total disagreement rows: {len(result)}")

# View result
result

























Total disagreement rows: 14

Observation


    

  • Out of all 3 techniques, CoT performance (least variation) is the most robust between V1 and V2.
    

    • 

  • Zero-shot and few-shot output slightly more positive sentiment predictions given that our sample has more high-rating records than low-rating records. CoT outputs the most balanced predictions and most number of Neutral predictions. Zero-shot & few-shot tend have less Neutral results and CoT.
    

    • 

  • Zero-shot and Few-shot (V1+V2), tend to predict less Neutral value when there is a mix of positive and negative feedbacks. CoT seems to be able to identify mixed sentiments and thus results more Neutral values, suggesting stronger ability to handle nuances.
    

    Example (index=19121):
In this incomplete review, the customer is apparently not satisfied regardless of the compliments at the beginning. The rating of 3 also suggests that dominant tone should be negative. Both zero-shot and few-shot give the wrong classification of "Neutral".
    

    Example (index=22401):
In this review, the sentiment should be "Neutral" even with a rating of 4 because "Fit" is the actual issue regardless of all the compliments.  But both zero-shot and few-shot give the wrong classification of "Positive".
    

    • 























Sentiment Prediction Comparison of Prompting Techniques:






















Sentiment Prediction Comparison
We now use LLM to compare sentiment prediction performance across the three prompting techniques.


















In [74]:






sentiment_comparison_prompt = ChatPromptTemplate.from_template(
"""
You are an expert in evaluating LLM prompting strategies for sentiment analysis.

Your task is to compare outputs from different prompting techniques (Zero-shot, Few-shot, and Chain-of-Thought) and determine which produces the most accurate and reliable outcome.

You must evaluate each prediction independently using ONLY the review text as ground truth.

Do NOT assume any prediction is correct.
Do NOT merge predictions.
Do NOT reuse reasoning from one prediction to justify another.

Focus on:
- alignment with the review text
- correctness of sentiment
- handling of mixed sentiment
- overall reliability of the prediction

You must also select the single best overall outcome among the three techniques.

INPUT:

Review: {review_text}

Predicted Sentiments:

- Zero-shot: {zero_shot_prediction}
- Few-shot: {few_shot_prediction}
- CoT: {cot_prediction}

====================================================
TASK
====================================================

Step 1: Infer the TRUE sentiment from the review.

Use these rules:
- Positive → clear satisfaction, praise, recommendation
- Negative → complaint, dissatisfaction, frustration
- Neutral → mixed sentiment OR no dominant emotion

Text sentiment ALWAYS overrides rating assumptions.

----------------------------------------------------

Step 2: Evaluate each prediction independently

For each method (Zero-shot, Few-shot, CoT):

- Compare prediction with the inferred true sentiment
- Assign a score:
  - 1.0 → exact match
  - 0.5 → partially correct (mixed sentiment misclassified as neutral, etc.)
  - 0.0 → incorrect

----------------------------------------------------

Step 3: Select the best technique

- Choose the method with the highest score
- If tie → prefer the one that better captures nuance (mixed sentiment, tone)

----------------------------------------------------

Step 4: Provide reasoning

- Briefly explain:
  - why the true sentiment was chosen
  - why the best method is better than the others

====================================================
OUTPUT FORMAT (STRICT JSON ONLY)
====================================================

{{
  "TrueSentiment": "Positive | Negative | Neutral",
  "Scores": {{
    "ZeroShot": 0.0,
    "FewShot": 0.0,
    "CoT": 0.0
  }},
  "BestMethod": "ZeroShot | FewShot | CoT",
  "Reason": "Concise explanation of decision"
}}
""")





















In [75]:






# create a dataframe to include sentiment predictions for all three techniques using v2 results
df_sentiment_comparison = df_zero_shot_result_v2[["Review", "Sentiment", "original_index"]].rename(columns={"Sentiment": "ZeroShotPrediction"})
df_sentiment_comparison = df_sentiment_comparison.merge(
    df_few_shot_result_v2[["Sentiment", "original_index"]].rename(columns={"Sentiment": "FewShotPrediction"}),
    on="original_index",how="inner"
)
df_sentiment_comparison = df_sentiment_comparison.merge(
    df_CoT_result_v2[["Sentiment", "original_index"]].rename(columns={"Sentiment": "CoTPrediction"}),
    on="original_index",how="inner"
)





















In [76]:






review_comparison_input_column_map = {
    "Review": "review_text",
    "ZeroShotPrediction": "zero_shot_prediction",
    "FewShotPrediction": "few_shot_prediction",
    "CoTPrediction": "cot_prediction",
}
review_comparison_input_default_map = {
    "review_text": "[No comment]" # defalut value if no review
}
# create ReviewAnalysisAgent instance and set rate limit.
parser = JsonOutputParser()
review_comparison_agent = ReviewAnalysisAgent(llm, parser, review_comparison_input_column_map, review_comparison_input_default_map, calls=1, period=3)





















In [ ]:






with ProcessTimer("Comparing sentiment prediction across three techniques based on review text only"):
    df_sentiment_comparison_result = review_comparison_agent.process_df_with_progress(df_sentiment_comparison, sentiment_comparison_prompt, show_progress=True)

























Processing reviews:   0%|          | 0/100 [00:00<?, ?it/s]












Comparing sentiment prediction across three techniques based on review text only took 122.934256 seconds

# save the result dataframe
path_sentiment_comparison_result = path.join(project_root,'GenAIReviewCategorization','sentiment_comparison_result.tsv')
df_sentiment_comparison_result.to_csv(path_sentiment_comparison_result, index=False, sep="\t", quoting=csv.QUOTE_MINIMAL)
# load saved df_sentiment_comparison_result

In [78]:






# average scores
import scipy.stats as stats
df_sentiment_comparison_result[["Scores.ZeroShot","Scores.FewShot","Scores.CoT"]].mean()
scores_zero_shot = df_sentiment_comparison_result["Scores.ZeroShot"]
scores_few_shot  = df_sentiment_comparison_result["Scores.FewShot"]
scores_CoT   = df_sentiment_comparison_result["Scores.CoT"]
t_stat_1, p_val_1_two_sided = stats.ttest_rel(scores_zero_shot, scores_few_shot)
# Convert to one-sided p-value
p_val_1 = p_val_1_two_sided / 2 if t_stat_1 < 0 else 1 - (p_val_1_two_sided / 2)
print("Test 1: ZeroShot < FewShot")
print("t-stat:", t_stat_1)
print("p-value:", p_val_1)

t_stat_2, p_val_2_two_sided = stats.ttest_rel(scores_few_shot, scores_CoT)
# Convert to one-sided p-value
p_val_2 = p_val_2_two_sided / 2 if t_stat_2 < 0 else 1 - (p_val_2_two_sided / 2)
print("\nTest 2: FewShot < CoT")
print("t-stat:", t_stat_2)
print("p-value:", p_val_2)

























Test 1: ZeroShot < FewShot
t-stat: -2.6791054808659878
p-value: 0.00432272846032812

Test 2: FewShot < CoT
t-stat: -2.1712405933672376
p-value: 0.016151246875137165

























Conclusion


    

  • reject H₀ and have strong evidence that
FewShot performs significantly better than ZeroShot and CoT performs significantly better than CoT.
    

    • 

  • Per LLM judge, Chain-of-Thought clearly performs the best in sentiment prediction.
    

    • 























Observations and Insights






















Refined Insights:


Most meaningful and recurring insights from the customer reviews, as identified in all models.


    

  • Size is the most common issue in the sampled low-rating reviews.
    • 

  • Negative sentiment and low-rating leads to high urgency for retail insight.
    • 

  • Predicted sentiment is negatively correlated with rating, the lower rating the higher chance of Negative sentiment.
    • 

  • Sentiment is also highly correlated with Urgency. Negative sentiment often results high urgency. This is acutally consistent with what is prescribed in all prompts of different techniques.
    • 























LLM to Generate Actionable Product Improvement Suggestions






















Based on the aggregated insights from your best model, what are 3 short-term (3-6 months) and 3 long-term (6-12 months) actionable business recommendations for the retail company?
We use LLM to generate business strategy based on findings.


















In [79]:






business_strategy_system_prompt = """
You are a senior retail business strategist specializing in customer experience, product optimization, and AI-driven insights.

Your role is to translate analytical findings (including LLM prompt evaluation results and customer review insights) into clear, practical business strategies.

Focus on:
- revenue impact
- customer satisfaction
- operational feasibility
- prioritization and actionability

Avoid generic advice. Ground every recommendation in the provided findings.

Be concise, structured, and business-oriented.
"""

business_strategy_user_prompt = """
INPUT:

Business Context:
<<<{business_context}>>>

Business Objective:
<<<{business_objective}>>>

Prompt Technique Evaluation Findings:
<<<{prompt_evaluation_findings}>>>
# (e.g., CoT improves sentiment accuracy, Few-shot improves consistency, Zero-shot is fastest but less reliable)

Customer Review Findings:
<<<{customer_review_findings}>>>
# (e.g., common complaints, praise patterns, category issues)

====================================================
TASK
====================================================

Step 1: Synthesize Key Insights
- Identify 2–4 most important business-relevant insights
- Link prompt performance to business impact (e.g., better sentiment → better CX decisions)

Step 2: Identify Opportunity Areas
- Customer experience improvements
- Product or merchandising changes
- Operational or process improvements
- AI/LLM deployment opportunities

Step 3: Generate Actionable Recommendations

Provide:
- 3 Short-Term recommendations (3–6 months)
- 3 Long-Term recommendations (6–12 months)

Each recommendation MUST include:
- Action (what to do)
- Rationale (why it matters based on inputs)
- Expected impact (business outcome)
- Priority (High / Medium / Low)

Guidelines:
- Be specific and actionable (not generic)
- Tie directly to findings
- Balance quick wins vs strategic investments
- Avoid repeating the same idea in different wording

====================================================
OUTPUT FORMAT (STRICT)
====================================================

{{
  "KeyInsights": [
    "...",
    "...",
    "..."
  ],
  "ShortTermRecommendations": [
    {{
      "Action": "...",
      "Rationale": "...",
      "ExpectedImpact": "...",
      "Priority": "High | Medium | Low"
    }},
    {{
      "Action": "...",
      "Rationale": "...",
      "ExpectedImpact": "...",
      "Priority": "High | Medium | Low"
    }},
    {{
      "Action": "...",
      "Rationale": "...",
      "ExpectedImpact": "...",
      "Priority": "High | Medium | Low"
    }}
  ],
  "LongTermRecommendations": [
    {{
      "Action": "...",
      "Rationale": "...",
      "ExpectedImpact": "...",
      "Priority": "High | Medium | Low"
    }},
    {{
      "Action": "...",
      "Rationale": "...",
      "ExpectedImpact": "...",
      "Priority": "High | Medium | Low"
    }},
    {{
      "Action": "...",
      "Rationale": "...",
      "ExpectedImpact": "...",
      "Priority": "High | Medium | Low"
    }}
  ]
}}"""





















In [80]:






business_context="""
ChicStyle — a growing fashion retail platform — experiences massive spikes in customer activity during festive seasons and holiday sales. As people buy clothing and accessories for celebrations, the volume of incoming reviews increases drastically. These reviews pour in every hour, ranging from positive praise to urgent complaints about fit, delivery delays, product defects, or sizing issues.During such high-pressure periods, even a slight delay in reading or responding to customer feedback can have serious consequences. If the retail team fails to take quick action, customers may feel ignored during an emotionally significant time (festive purchases), leading to frustration, spoiled shopping experiences, and ultimately reduced trust in the brand. This not only results in immediate business loss but also impacts repeat purchases and long-term loyalty. To avoid customer dissatisfaction and protect brand reputation during peak sales periods, ChicStyle needs a system that can process thousands of reviews instantly, accurately, and with business context.
"""

business_objective="""
Build a Generative AI feedback system that uses prompt engineering(Zero-Shot,Few-Shot,and Chain-of-Thought prompting)to:

* Analyze and categorize sentiment in real time.
* Detect which product or service each feedback refers to.
* Summarize insights by product category and urgency level.
* Automatically send short personalized messages to customers based on sentiment—thanking them for positive feedback, acknowledging neutral comments,and apologizing for negative ones while informing them that a team member will reach out soon.
* Generate short, actionable reports for retail teams.This intelligent system will help retailers take quick action on customer issues,improve product quality faster,and enhance customer satisfaction—turning massive unstructured feedback into meaningful,real-time business intelligence.
"""

prompt_evaluation_findings = """
LLM-judge evaluation score (v1 vs v2)

| Model            | V1     | V2     |Final   |
|----------------- |--------|--------|--------|
| RAG + CoT        |   x    |   x    | 0.979  |
| Chain-of-Thought | 0.784  | 0.932  | 0.939  |
| Few-shot         | 0.780  | 0.929  |   x    |
| Zero-shot        | 0.772  | 0.906  |   x    |

When no reference is provided, the LLM judge compares predictions against internally inferred values based on the judge prompt. A prediction prompt that is structurally closer to the judge’s inference logic will likely score higher. Thus, a reference-free LLM judge is more suitable for pairwise comparison between different versions of the same technique. To establish an absolute baseline across different techniques, a ground truth dataset is a better approach. However, a ground truth approach is not scalable with a large number of reviews unless there is an existing historical response dataset that can serve as ground truth.

* **How do the techniques (Zero-Shot, Few-Shot, CoT, RAG+CoT) compare in terms of their responses? Use LLM to give verdict?**
    * For each technique, V2 is usually better than V1, which is expected due to more structured rules.
    * Overall performance: **RAG + CoT > CoT > Few-Shot > Zero-Shot**.

* **Which technique was the most reliable and consistent? Why do you think it performed the best?**

  Out of all techniques, **RAG + CoT is the best**, followed by **CoT**. CoT forces intermediate structure. Without CoT, models often jump straight to an answer. That increases:

  * shortcut reasoning
  * pattern matching
  * hidden assumption errors

  CoT breaks this into steps like:

  `infer → compare → decide → explain`

  **RAG further improves performance** by adding grounding from retrieved reference data. This:

  * reduces hallucination
  * improves consistency across similar inputs
  * aligns outputs with expected patterns used in evaluation

  The combination of **RAG (grounding) + CoT (structured reasoning)** leads to the most reliable and consistent results.

* **What model and prompt design would you propose for a production environment?**

    * Based on the above experiment and analysis, **RAG + CoT is recommended for a production environment**.
    * Proposed design:
        * Use a strong LLM with **Chain-of-Thought structured prompting**
        * Add a **retrieval layer (RAG)** using historical labeled reviews or responses
        * Pipeline:
            * Input → Retrieve (RAG) → CoT reasoning → Structured Output
    * This approach provides:
        * higher accuracy
        * better consistency
        * stronger alignment with business rules
        * scalability when reference data is available
"""


customer_review_findings = """
Most meaningful and recurring insights from the customer reviews, as identified in all models.

1. Size is the most common issue in the sampled low-rating reviews.
2. Negative sentiment and low-rating leads to high urgency for retail insight.
3. Predicted sentiment is negatively correlated with rating, the lower rating the higher chance of Negative sentiment.
4. Sentiment is also highly correlated with Urgency. Negative sentiment often results high urgency. This is acutally consistent with what is prescribed in all prompts of different techniques.
"""





















In [81]:






# Call the LLM with the business_prompt to generate strategic recommendations.
messages=[
    {"role": "system", "content": business_strategy_system_prompt },
    {"role": "user", "content": business_strategy_user_prompt}
]
business_strategy_prompt = ChatPromptTemplate.from_messages(messages)
business_strategy_llm_chain =  business_strategy_prompt | llm | parser
business_strategy_result = business_strategy_llm_chain.invoke(
    {"business_context": business_context,
     "business_objective": business_objective,
     "prompt_evaluation_findings": prompt_evaluation_findings,
     "customer_review_findings": customer_review_findings})

High




2
Develop a closed-loop feedback system that tracks resolution outcomes for high-urgency cases and uses this data to refine AI triage and escalation logic.
Continuous learning from real outcomes will optimize the system’s prioritization and improve both operational efficiency and customer experience.
Drives ongoing improvement in response quality, reduces manual workload, and increases customer loyalty.
Medium











































Observations and Insights
    

  • We use a reference-free llm-judge for evaluation. A more robust approach is to use ground truth to establish a baseline and to use historical response dataset to continously evaluate and update pipeline/prompts to improve prediction quality. Evaluation with ground truth is more robust and objective with low variance.
    • 

  • In the short-term (3-6 month), using GenAI pipeline to assist in processing reviews with human supervision while collecting curated and approved human responses.
    • 

  • In the long term (6-12 month), can use actual resolution outcomes (ground truth) to build a RAG system to further ground the responses of GenAI pipeline and fully automate the whole process.
    • 























How does this automated GenAI pipeline solve the initial business problem and create value?






















Observations and Insights
    

  • The automated GenAI pipeline is able to quicky identify review Category(feedback types), gauge review sentiment and sense of urgency and create proper personalized message for post-sale team to review or even competely automate the process for sending high-urgency responses, thus improving turn-around time and customer care quality.
    • 























Final Proposed Solution
1. Chain-of-Thought Review Intelligence Engine
Uses CoT prompting to improve accuracy for:


    

  • Mixed sentiment reviews
    

    • 

  • Product-fit complaints
    

    • 

  • Quality issues
    

    • 

  • Delivery delays
    

    • 

  • Urgent complaints
    

    • 

  • Personalized responses
    

    • 





2. RAG-Grounded Response Layer
    

  • Uses curated historical review-response pairs to retrieve similar prior cases
    

    • 

  • Generates grounded, brand-consistent responses
    

    • 





3. Real-Time Dashboard
Surfaces:


    

  • Sentiment trends
    

    • 

  • Urgent complaints
    

    • 

  • Top product issues
    

    • 

  • Category-level insights
    

    • 

  • Customer response status
    

    • 





4. Human-in-the-Loop Escalation
    

  • High-urgency reviews are routed to support or product teams before automated action
    • 























Executive Summary
ChicStyle should adopt a Chain-of-Thought–driven Generative AI feedback system to process high-volume customer reviews during peak retail seasons.


The proposed model uses structured reasoning to classify sentiment, detect product or service issues, summarize customer feedback, assign urgency, and generate personalized responses. Compared with simpler prompting approaches, Chain-of-Thought is better suited for complex retail reviews because it can handle mixed sentiment, distinguish primary versus secondary issues, and produce more reliable business-ready insights.


To improve reliability and brand consistency, the system should include a Retrieval-Augmented Generation layer using curated historical customer service responses. This grounds AI-generated messages in approved past responses and reduces hallucination risk.






















Recommended for Implementation
Short-Term (3–6 Months)
    

  • Deploy CoT-based sentiment and issue classification
    

      

    • Use structured internal reasoning for sentiment, category, urgency, and summary generation
      • 

    • Expected benefit: Faster and more accurate review triage
      • 

    

    • 

  • Add RAG-grounded personalized responses
    

      

    • Build a vector database of curated historical complaints and approved responses
      • 

    • Expected benefit: More consistent, brand-safe customer communication
      • 

    

    • 

  • Launch a real-time review intelligence dashboard
    

      

    • Track negative sentiment, urgency, recurring issues, and product-level risks
      • 

    • Expected benefit: Faster operational and merchandising decisions
      • 

    

    • 





Long-Term (6–12 Months)
    

  • Integrate feedback intelligence into CRM and support workflows
    

      

    • Auto-route high-urgency issues to the right team
      • 

    • Expected benefit: Shorter resolution cycles
      • 

    

    • 

  • Build product-quality trend detection
    

      

    • Identify recurring fit, fabric, sizing, and delivery issues by product class
      • 

    • Expected benefit: Reduce return rates and improve assortment decisions
      • 

    

    • 

  • Create continuous learning and governance loop
    

      

    • Monitor accuracy, response quality, escalation outcomes, and customer satisfaction
      • 

    • Expected benefit: System improves over time while reducing operational risk
      • 

    

    • 























Model Specification
ChicStyle should implement a Chain-of-Thought Generative AI feedback intelligence system as the primary model design.


The system will use structured internal reasoning to:


    

  1. Understand the review context.
    2. 

  2. Identify product/service issue.
    3. 

  3. Classify sentiment.
    4. 

  4. Categorize issue type.
    5. 

  5. Determine urgency.
    6. 

  6. Generate concise summary.
    7. 

  7. Produce personalized customer response.
    8. 



The model will think step-by-step internally but return only structured business outputs.






















Architecture based on our solution suggestion

Review Pipeline Harness
Hight level workflow defined by harness framework
    

  • The following code is ONLY for the purpose of demo how workflow can be done in harness framework.
    • 

  • The code is incomplete.
    • 



















In [72]:






@dataclass
class ReviewPipelineHarness:
    rag_agent: Any
    analysis_agent: Any
    judge_agent: Any
    judge_validator: Any
    escalation_agent: Any

    def run_one(self, row: Dict[str, Any]) -> Dict[str, Any]:
        review_input = build_review_input(row)

        reference_input = self.rag_agent.run(review_input)

        prediction = self.analysis_agent.run(
            review_input=review_input,
            reference_input=reference_input,
        )

        escalation = self.escalation_agent.run(
            prediction=prediction
        )

        return {
            "review_input": review_input,
            "reference_input": reference_input,
            "prediction": prediction,
            "escalation": escalation,
        }

    def run_dataframe(self, df):
        rows = []

        for _, row in df.iterrows():
            result = self.run_one(row.to_dict())

            prediction = result["prediction"]
            judge = result["judge_result"]
            escalation = result["escalation"]

            retail = prediction.get("RetailInsight", {})
            scores = judge.get("scores", {})

            rows.append({
                **row.to_dict(),
                "Category": prediction.get("Category", ""),
                "Summary": prediction.get("Summary", ""),
                "Sentiment": prediction.get("Sentiment", ""),
                "Urgency": retail.get("Urgency", ""),
                "PersonalizedMessage": prediction.get("PersonalizedMessage", ""),
                "Judge_FinalScore": judge.get("final_score", ""),
                "Judge_Category": scores.get("Category", ""),
                "Judge_Summary": scores.get("Summary", ""),
                "Judge_Sentiment": scores.get("Sentiment", ""),
                "Judge_Urgency": scores.get("RetailInsight", ""),
                "Judge_PersonalizedMessage": scores.get("PersonalizedMessage", ""),
                "HumanReviewRequired": escalation.get("HumanReviewRequired", ""),
                "EscalationReasons": "; ".join(escalation.get("EscalationReasons", [])),
            })

        import pandas as pd
        return pd.DataFrame(rows)

# running the pipeline using pipeline harness
review_harness = ReviewPipelineHarness(
    rag_agent=RAGRetrievalAgent(
        vectorstore=vector_store,
        k=3,
        similarity_threshold=0.80,
    ),
    analysis_agent=ReviewAnalysisAgent(
        llm=llm,
        prompt_template=rag_cot_prompt_template,
    ),
    # agent to escalate urgency based on retail insight urgency prediction
    escalation_agent=EscalationAgent(min_score_threshold=0.80),
)

agentic_results_df = review_harness.run_dataframe(df_sample)

























Risks & Challenges
    

  1. Model Misclassification Risk
    2. 



    

  • Incorrect sentiment → wrong action
    • 

  • Mitigation: human-in-the-loop for high urgency cases
    • 



    

  1. Cost Scalability
    2. 



    

  • High API usage during peak seasons
    • 

  • Mitigation: hybrid prompt strategy
    • 



    

  1. Integration Complexity
    2. 



    

  • Connecting with CRM, support, product systems
    • 

  • Mitigation: phased rollout
    • 























💰 Estimated Benefits vs Costs
📈 Benefits
    

  • Increased customer retention: +5–10%
    • 

  • Reduced return rates: –10–15%
    • 

  • Faster issue resolution: 2–3x improvement
    • 

  • Operational efficiency gains: 30–50% reduction in manual work
    • 



💸 Estimated Costs


Development Costs


    

  • One-time pipeline development and testing: approximately 3,000–8,000 for a pilot implementation.
    • 

  • Dashboard and reporting setup: approximately 1,000–3,000 depending on integration depth.
    • 

  • RAG knowledge-base preparation and vector index setup: approximately 2,000–6,000 depending on data quality and volume.
    • 



Operational Costs


    

  • LLM API usage: approximately 200–1,000 per month depending on review volume, model choice, and prompt size.
    • 

  • Cloud storage and compute: approximately 100–300 per month for pilot to early-scale usage.
    • 

  • Ongoing monitoring and prompt maintenance: approximately 100–500 per month depending on governance requirements.
    • 



Estimated per-review processing cost is approximately 0.002–0.01 for lightweight classification and generation workflows, depending on prompt length, model selection, and whether retrieval context is included.






















Final Takeaway
ChicStyle’s competitive advantage will come from:
    

  • Turning real-time customer feedback into immediate, intelligent action
    

    • 

  • By leveraging Generative AI + optimized prompting strategies, the company can:
    

      

    • Respond faster
      • 

    • Fix issues earlier
      • 

    • Delight customers consistently
      • 

    

    • 

  • Ultimately driving revenue growth, loyalty, and brand strength.
    

    • 























Conclusion
    

  • When fixed rules do not work well along with limited training data, GenAI models are better option than tranditional ML models to efficiently handle business complexity and ambiguity.
    • 

  • A GenAI solution can potentially cost more than traditional models due to costly LLM service provider cost.(Had to switch to paid personal account due to running out of quota with the given key.)
    •
